title: Bundle-Stable Store-Only EX/MEM Write Data
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The structural slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto
free win: preserve CoreMark cycles while reducing routing, LUT4, or median
Fmax without changing packed bundle widths or the memory-ordering contract.

The most successful recent structural pattern was bundle-stable dead
metadata constantization: `ex_mem_t.branch_taken` and `branch_target` were
kept in the packed bundle but driven to constants because no downstream
stage consumed them. A similar narrower opportunity remains on the EX/MEM
store-data rail. `ex_mem_t.write_data` is only semantically live for store
instructions, where MEM uses it to form byte-lane write data, pending-store
payloads, and RVFI `mem_wdata`. For ALU, branch, jump, load, fence, illegal,
and divide instructions, the field is downstream-dead because `mem_wmask`
is zero and the dmem write enable is deasserted.

A previous structural attempt removed the `write_data` field entirely and
reused `rs2_val`, but changing the bundle/data-fanout shape was
placement-noisy. This hypothesis keeps the EX/MEM field layout stable and
only prevents non-store instructions from carrying a live forwarded-rs2
value on a store-only payload rail.
hypothesis:
Keep `ex_mem_t.write_data` in the bundle, but make it a store-qualified
payload at the EX/MEM boundary. In the normal EX path, drive the field with
the post-forwarded `rs2` value only when the instruction is a valid store
candidate:

`store_payload_live = in.valid && !in.ctrl.is_illegal && in.ctrl.mem_write`

`reg_q.write_data <= store_payload_live ? rs2 : 32'b0`

On the divide-complete path and on long-operation bubbles, drive
`write_data` to zero as today-equivalent dead metadata. Keep `rs2_val`
unchanged for RVFI source-data reporting, branch comparisons, divider shadow
operands, and any other architectural source-value use.

MEM-stage behavior must remain bit-identical for real stores: byte and
halfword replication, live `io_dmemWData`, pending-store enqueue data, and
RVFI `mem_wdata` all still come from `in.write_data` when `in.ctrl.mem_write`
and `mem_access` are true. For non-store instructions, `io_dmemWEn` and
RVFI `mem_wmask` remain zero, so forcing the dead write-data rail to zero is
architecturally invisible and only removes useless rs2 fanout into the
backend.

This is intentionally narrower than removing the field or unifying it with
`rs2_val`. It preserves packed struct widths, module ports, forwarding
priority, load-use timing, branch/JAL/JALR repair, replay prediction,
pending-store behavior, dmem handshakes, divider latency, writeback timing,
top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0 retirement ordering,
channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder illegal-default discipline,
memory traps, and CoreMark timing markers.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should be identical because no scheduling,
handshake, PC, writeback, or retirement rule changes. The intended Pareto
win is a small LUT4 reduction and/or higher median Fmax by removing a
duplicate 32-bit forwarded-rs2 payload from all non-store EX/MEM updates
while keeping the successful bundle-stable topology.

changes.description:
Qualify the EX/MEM `write_data` payload at the stage boundary. In the
normal execute path, assign the post-forwarded `rs2` value only for
valid, non-illegal store instructions; otherwise assign `32'b0`. In the
divide-complete path, assign `write_data` to `32'b0` because DIV/REM do
not store. Preserve `rs2_val`, operand forwarding, branch and jump target
behavior, misaligned-target trap gating, divider launch/completion,
EX/MEM valid and stall behavior, JAL/JALR link-data routing, and all
RVFI-visible semantics.

Keep store-data consumption semantics unchanged, but audit comments and
any local store-data helper names so it is clear that `in.write_data` is
meaningful only when `in.ctrl.mem_write` is true. Preserve byte/halfword
replication, memory masks, misalignment traps, `mem_access` gating,
store-slot enqueue and wait behavior, MEM/WB hold semantics, RVFI
memory payload fields, module ports, and all external behavior.

Update comments only to document that `ex_mem_t.write_data` is retained
as a bundle-stable store-only payload, while `rs2_val` remains the
RVFI/source-value rail for every instruction. Do not change bundle field
names or widths, ALU encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field
widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- `rtl/ex_stage.sv`: added `store_payload_live = in.valid && !in.ctrl.is_illegal && in.ctrl.mem_write` at the EX/MEM boundary. Normal EX updates now drive `reg_q.write_data` with forwarded `rs2` only for live legal stores; all other instructions drive `32'b0`.
- `rtl/ex_stage.sv`: divide-complete updates now drive `reg_q.write_data` to `32'b0`. Divider source reporting remains on `rs1_val` / `rs2_val`.
- `rtl/mem_stage.sv`: kept store-data behavior unchanged for real stores, but renamed the local replicated payload helper from `wdata_rep` to `store_wdata_rep` and updated comments to document that `in.write_data` is store-only.
- `rtl/core_pkg.sv`: updated `ex_mem_t` comments only. The packed field names and widths are unchanged.

## Deviations

- No functional deviation from the hypothesis. The EX/MEM bundle layout, MEM consumption points, RVFI source-value rails, and memory handshake behavior are unchanged.

## Concerns

- None known. For non-store instructions, `mem_wmask` remains zero, so the forced-zero write-data rail is architecturally dead.

## Local verification status

- Lint: pass.
  - Command: `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`
- Formal: pass via a temporary writable copy.
  - Direct command attempted: `bash formal/run_all.sh`
  - Direct run did not reach RTL checks because this worktree's `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink outside the writable sandbox, and the script failed while clearing staged `.sv` files with `Operation not permitted`.
  - Workaround used for local self-check: copied `rtl/`, `formal/run_all.sh`, `formal/checks.cfg`, `formal/checks-deep.cfg`, `formal/wrapper.sv`, and a dereferenced `formal/riscv-formal/` tree to `/tmp/auto-arch-formal.PXVsiD`, then ran `bash formal/run_all.sh` there.
  - Result: `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Skewed Second-Way Replay Index
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The predictor slot therefore needs a strict Pareto free win:
preserve the successful static branch/direct-JAL predictor and two-bank
replay frontend while improving CoreMark cycles or keeping cycles flat
with no area regression.

The experiment history makes the boundary clear. Removing the replay
predictor exposes too much random iStall penalty, and adding direction
tables or return prediction tends to spend too much LUT/Fmax. But the
replay table still has a predictor-local weakness: both ways are indexed
by exactly pc[8:2], so all instruction words separated by 512-byte
multiples compete in the same two-entry set. Direct-mapping or changing
replacement policy lost hit rate, which suggests the associativity should
stay, but the alias pattern itself can be improved.

A skewed second way keeps the same storage budget and full tag protection
while reducing deterministic replay conflicts. Bank 0 can remain indexed
by pc[8:2] for the current fast/common placement. Bank 1 can use a cheap
XOR of the same low index with high PC/tag bits, so a third hot word that
conflicts in bank 0 has a better chance of landing in a different bank-1
slot. The full replay candidate PC guard still prevents wrong-instruction
use; a collision can only become a replay miss.
hypothesis:
Change only the indexing of the second IF replay bank. Keep bank 0 exactly
as today, with index pc[8:2] and tag pc[31:9]. For bank 1, derive a skewed
index from the same word address, for example:

`bank1_idx = pc[8:2] ^ pc[15:9]`

or an equivalent low-cost XOR of the primary index and high tag bits.
Bank 1 should continue storing the full pc[31:9] tag and instruction word.

On lookup, compare bank 0 at the primary lookup index and bank 1 at the
skewed lookup index. On fill, check for a matching tag in bank 0 at the
primary fill index and in bank 1 at the skewed fill index. If either way
already matches, refresh that way. If neither matches, keep the existing
one-bit MRU victim policy indexed by the primary pc[8:2] set: an MRU value
chooses whether to replace bank 0 at the primary index or bank 1 at the
skewed index, and the MRU bit is updated to the bank actually filled.

Preserve the rest of the replay predictor exactly. Both banks stay
128-entry resetless tag/instruction arrays behind reset-cleared valid bits.
The candidate PC equality guard remains full 32-bit. Live imem data still
wins over replay data, and same-entry fill bypass must account for the
selected bank and its corresponding index/tag so it never bypasses across a
skewed alias. The static backward-branch predictor, aligned direct-JAL
prediction, JALR EX resolution, redirect priority, fetch_ready semantics,
PC update behavior, and outcome-only prediction metadata all remain
unchanged.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should improve if the current replay misses are
caused by more than two hot instructions aliasing on pc[8:2], while LUT4
should stay essentially flat because table capacity, tag width, valid bits,
instruction payloads, and MRU state do not increase. The intended Pareto
win is a small reduction in imem-stall bubbles without weakening any
CLAUDE.md invariant or changing top-level IO.

changes.description:
Add separate primary and bank-1-skewed replay indexes for fill and
lookup. Keep bank 0 indexed by pc[8:2]. Index bank 1 with a cheap XOR
of pc[8:2] and high tag bits, while keeping full pc[31:9] tags in both
banks. Update lookup hit compares, fill-bank match compares, selected
bank writes, valid-bit writes, MRU updates, and fill-bypass equality to
use the correct per-bank index. Preserve both replay banks, tag and
instruction payload formats, reset behavior, live-imem priority,
candidate-PC guard, static branch/direct-JAL prediction, JALR handling,
redirect priority, PC update semantics, and fetch_ready behavior.

Update only IF/ID replay predictor comments to document that bank 0 uses
the primary pc[8:2] index while bank 1 uses a skewed index to reduce
deterministic replay conflicts. Do not change bundle fields, control
encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO
assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Updated `rtl/if_stage.sv` so replay bank 0 keeps the primary `pc[8:2]` index.
- Added a skewed second-bank index using `pc[8:2] ^ pc[15:9]` for both lookup and fill.
- Kept full `pc[31:9]` tag storage and comparison in both replay banks.
- Kept the existing per-primary-index MRU replacement policy; the MRU bit still records the bank actually filled.
- Updated fill match checks, lookup hit checks, selected-bank writes, valid-bit writes, and fill bypass logic to use the selected bank's corresponding index.
- Updated the replay predictor comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv`.

## Deviations

- No functional deviations from the hypothesis. The skew expression is the proposed `pc[8:2] ^ pc[15:9]`.
- No top-level IO, pipeline bundle fields, predictor metadata, static branch/JAL prediction, JALR handling, redirect priority, PC update behavior, or reset behavior was changed.

## Concerns

- None known. The same-entry fill bypass is intentionally bank-aware so a live imem fill only bypasses into the registered candidate when the selected write bank and that bank's index/tag match the lookup entry.

## Local verification status

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: the direct in-worktree run did not reach proofs because this sandbox denied deletion of staged files through the `formal/riscv-formal` symlink (`Operation not permitted`).
- Formal workaround: copied the edited RTL plus the same formal script/config/wrapper and riscv-formal tree to `/tmp/r26s2-formal`, then ran `bash formal/run_all.sh` there. Result: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Shared MEM Access Fire Qualifiers
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The micro_opt slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto free
win: preserve CoreMark cycles while shaving a little MEM-stage control
logic or improving median Fmax.

The best recent micro win came from a narrow, behavior-preserving
source-shape cleanup: constantizing dead EX/MEM branch metadata without
changing packed bundle widths or scheduling. Larger attempts to rewrite
replay state, control payloads, reset structure, and arithmetic units have
been placement-sensitive. The next most promising angle is therefore a
small local factoring in an already-successful area: the MEM-stage access
and RVFI mask control.

`rtl/mem_stage.sv` repeatedly recomputes the same qualified access
predicates. The expression `mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_write` appears in
the live dmem write enable, store-bufferability test, MEM wait decision,
and RVFI write-mask generation. The analogous load predicate appears in
dmem read enable, MEM wait, and RVFI read-mask generation. These are
single properties of the MEM-stage instruction, not independent decisions
at each consumer. Sharing them explicitly should reduce duplicated AND
logic and control fanout without changing any data path or state.
hypothesis:
Add shared MEM-stage access-fire qualifiers:

`mem_load_access = mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_read;`

`mem_store_access = mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_write;`

Use `mem_store_access` for store bufferability, live `dmem_wen`, store-side
MEM wait selection, and MEM/WB `mem_wmask`. Use `mem_load_access` for live
`dmem_ren`, load-side MEM wait selection, and MEM/WB `mem_rmask`. Keep
`mem_access` itself as the common valid/legal/aligned memory-operation
guard, and keep `aligned_addr` gated exactly as today.

This is a source-shape optimization only. The byte/halfword/word mask
values, store-data replication, load extraction, memory misalignment trap
marking, pending-store enqueue rule, store-slot drain priority, MEM/WB hold
behavior, RVFI memory address/data fields, and dmem port behavior must
remain bit-identical. In particular, legal normal-RAM stores still retire
into the pending-store slot under the same `!store_slot_valid &&
!dmem_ready` condition, loads still wait on `!dmem_ready`, nonbufferable
stores still wait for the live dmem handshake, and trapping or illegal
memory operations still suppress external enables and report zero masks.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because no pipeline
state, handshake rule, PC decision, replay behavior, forwarding path,
divider protocol, writeback rule, or retirement rule changes. The intended
Pareto win is a small LUT4 reduction and/or higher median Fmax from one
shared load qualifier and one shared store qualifier feeding the MEM-stage
control consumers, while preserving the already-met LUT target and every
CLAUDE.md invariant.

changes.description:
Add local `mem_load_access` and `mem_store_access` signals derived once
from `mem_access` plus the load/store control bits. Replace repeated
`mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_read` and `mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_write`
expressions in dmem enables, store-bufferability, MEM wait selection,
and MEM/WB RVFI mask generation with those shared qualifiers. Preserve
store-data replication, byte and halfword masks, load lane muxes,
misalignment traps, `mem_access` gating, store-slot enqueue and wait
semantics, MEM/WB hold behavior, RVFI memory payload fields, module
ports, and all externally visible behavior.

Update comments only if useful to document that MEM-stage load/store
access-fire qualifiers are local implementation helpers; they do not
change pipeline bundle fields, control encodings, decoder illegal
defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2
contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Added local `mem_load_access` and `mem_store_access` qualifiers in `rtl/mem_stage.sv`.
- Reused `mem_store_access` for store bufferability, live `dmem_wen`, store-side `mem_wait`, and MEM/WB `mem_wmask`.
- Reused `mem_load_access` for live `dmem_ren`, load-side `mem_wait`, and MEM/WB `mem_rmask`.
- Left `mem_access` as the common valid/legal/aligned memory-operation guard and kept `aligned_addr` gated by `mem_access`.

## Deviations

- No functional deviations from the hypothesis.
- No `core_pkg.sv` comment update was needed because the qualifiers are purely local MEM-stage implementation helpers and do not affect bundle fields or encodings.

## Concerns

- None beyond normal placement sensitivity for source-shape micro-optimizations.
- The change is intended to be bit-identical: store-data replication, byte masks, load extraction, misalignment trap behavior, pending-store enqueue, store-slot priority, MEM/WB hold behavior, RVFI payloads, and dmem port behavior are unchanged.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- Direct in-tree `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked before any RTL check by sandbox permissions while the script tried to clear staged files under `formal/riscv-formal/cores/auto-arch-researcher` (`Operation not permitted`).
- Equivalent local formal run staged under `/tmp/auto_arch_formal_run.Z77i7B` with the same edited `rtl/`, `formal/wrapper.sv`, `formal/checks.cfg`, and a real copied `riscv-formal` tree: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Bundle-Stable MEM/WB Final Result Projection
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The structural slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto free
win: preserve CoreMark cycles while reducing LUT4 or improving median Fmax
without changing top-level IO, memory ordering, or the RVFI contract.

The latest accepted structural win used a bundle-stable constantization
pattern: keep packed pipeline fields in place, but drive downstream-dead
metadata to deterministic constants so synthesis can prune fanout and flops
without the placement churn of changing struct widths. The MEM/WB result
boundary has a similar opportunity. `mem_wb_t` still carries both
`alu_result` and `read_data`, and `wb_stage.sv` selects between them with
`ctrl.mem_to_reg`. Downstream of MEM, however, the register file,
MEM/WB-to-EX forwarding, and RVFI `rd_wdata` all need only one final
architectural writeback value.

A prior structural attempt removed the two result fields and added a new
final writeback rail, which reduced state but changed the packed bundle
shape and was placement-noisy. This hypothesis keeps the stable MEM/WB
layout and repurposes the existing `read_data` rail as the final writeback
value, while driving the now-dead MEM/WB `alu_result` rail to a constant.
That should remove the WB-stage load-vs-ALU mux and a dead 32-bit payload
without changing the externally visible pipeline behavior.
hypothesis:
Keep the `mem_wb_t` field names and widths unchanged, but project the
final writeback value before the MEM/WB register. In `rtl/mem_stage.sv`,
compute a local final result:

`mem_wb_result = in.ctrl.mem_to_reg ? load_data : in.alu_result;`

Latch that value into `reg_q.read_data` for every normal MEM/WB update.
Drive `reg_q.alu_result` to `32'b0`, because no downstream stage consumes
the MEM/WB copy of the preselected ALU result once the final writeback
value has been registered. The EX/MEM `alu_result` remains unchanged and
continues to feed memory addresses, store-slot enqueue addresses, branch
metadata, and EX/MEM forwarding exactly as today.

Update `rtl/wb_stage.sv` so `w_data` is driven directly from
`in.read_data`, now documented as the final MEM/WB writeback-result rail.
Keep `w_en = in.ctrl.reg_write && in.valid` and keep `w_addr = in.rd`.
Leave the register file's x0 protection, top-level `wb_w_data` forwarding
path, and RVFI `rd_wdata` wiring unchanged. The existing `hold_wb` behavior
must remain exact: during a MEM wait, clear only `valid` and retain the
previous `read_data`, `rd`, and control fields so MEM/WB forwarding through
`wb_w_data` still works for a stalled dependent instruction.

This is intentionally narrower than adding or removing bundle fields. The
packed MEM/WB type, module ports, RVFI memory payload fields, dmem protocol,
store-slot behavior, load extraction, memory traps, branch/JAL/JALR repair,
replay prediction, divider protocol, forwarding priority, load-use
interlock, top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only retirement,
channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder illegal-default discipline,
and CoreMark timing markers remain unchanged.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should be identical because no scheduling,
handshake, PC, memory-ordering, writeback timing, or retirement rule
changes. The intended Pareto win is lower LUT4 and/or higher median Fmax
by moving the load-vs-ALU result selection before MEM/WB, removing the
WB-stage result mux, and constantizing the dead MEM/WB `alu_result` rail
while preserving the stable pipeline bundle shape.

changes.description:
Preselect the final MEM/WB writeback result in MEM. Add a local
`mem_wb_result` equal to the existing load data for load-result
instructions and to `in.alu_result` otherwise. Latch that value into
`reg_q.read_data`, and drive `reg_q.alu_result` to `32'b0` because the
MEM/WB copy is no longer consumed downstream. Preserve load extraction,
byte/halfword masks, misalignment traps, dmem request gating,
store-slot enqueue and wait behavior, MEM/WB hold semantics, RVFI
memory payload fields, module ports, and all external behavior.

Remove the WB-stage load-vs-ALU result mux by driving `w_data` directly
from `in.read_data`, now the final MEM/WB writeback value. Preserve the
write-enable condition, destination register output, regfile x0
behavior, and RVFI `rd_wdata` semantics through the existing top-level
`wb_w_data` path.

Update comments only to document that `mem_wb_t.read_data` is retained
as the bundle-stable final writeback-result rail, while
`mem_wb_t.alu_result` is retained for bundle-shape stability but driven
constant after MEM. Do not change bundle field names or widths, ALU
encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO
assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes

- `rtl/mem_stage.sv`: added `mem_wb_result`, selecting `load_data` when `in.ctrl.mem_to_reg` is set and `in.alu_result` otherwise. Normal MEM/WB updates now latch that final value into `reg_q.read_data` and drive `reg_q.alu_result` to `32'b0`.
- `rtl/wb_stage.sv`: removed the WB-stage load-vs-ALU mux. `w_data` now comes directly from `in.read_data`; `w_en` and `w_addr` behavior is unchanged.
- `rtl/core_pkg.sv`: updated MEM/WB bundle comments only. Field names and widths are unchanged.
- `rtl/core.sv` and `rtl/ex_stage.sv`: updated stale comments describing `wb_w_data` as a mux output.

## Deviations

- No functional deviations from the hypothesis. The packed `mem_wb_t` layout and module ports are unchanged.
- The direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` invocation was blocked by the local sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared tree outside the writable worktree, and the script needs to remove/copy staged files there. To avoid modifying `formal/`, I reran the same script from a writable `/tmp/auto-arch-formal-run` mirror with `rtl/` linked back to this worktree.

## Concerns

- No known RTL concerns. `hold_wb` still clears only `valid`; the held `read_data`, `rd`, and control fields remain available for MEM/WB forwarding during MEM waits.

## Verification

- Lint: pass.
  - `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`
- Local formal: pass via writable `/tmp` mirror.
  - Direct worktree command failed before proof due sandbox staging permissions, not a counterexample.
  - Mirrored run result: `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Shared Static Predictor Opcode Predecode
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The predictor slot therefore needs a strict Pareto free win:
preserve the successful replay frontend and static branch/direct-JAL
policy while shaving a little IF predictor logic or improving median Fmax.

The experiment history makes the useful predictor boundary clear. The
two-bank replay predictor should stay intact; attempts to remove, retime,
retag, direct-map, repack, or change replacement generally hurt hit rate,
LUT4, or placement. Larger direction predictors and return predictors also
spent too much area/Fmax for the small CoreMark cycle gain. The remaining
promising predictor angle is a small source-shape cleanup in the existing
decode-light static predictor.

IF currently recognizes BRANCH and JAL with two separate full opcode
equality checks even though those opcodes share a common prefix. BRANCH is
`7'b1100011` and JAL is `7'b1101111`; both have opcode bits `[6:4] =
3'b110` and `[1:0] = 2'b11`, and differ only in bits `[3:2]` (`00` for
BRANCH, `11` for JAL). Factoring this common predicate should reduce the
IF static predictor's opcode-compare cone without changing any prediction
decision or replay behavior.
hypothesis:
Replace the separate IF-stage BRANCH and JAL opcode equality checks with a
shared decode-light control-flow predecode. For example:

`static_cf_opcode = (fetch_instr[6:4] == 3'b110) && (fetch_instr[1:0] == 2'b11);`

`branch_opcode = static_cf_opcode && (fetch_instr[3:2] == 2'b00);`

`jal_opcode = static_cf_opcode && (fetch_instr[3:2] == 2'b11);`

Keep the existing branch funct3 legality guard, backward-branch sign test,
shared `predict_target` adder, target-alignment check, direct-JAL prediction
rule, JALR-unpredicted behavior, `fetch_kill` gating, and
`predicted_taken` metadata unchanged.

This is bit-equivalent to the current predictor. BRANCH and JAL are still
recognized only for their exact RV32I opcodes, all other opcodes remain
non-predicted by the static branch/JAL path, reserved branch funct3 values
still stay unpredicted and trap through the normal decoder/RVFI path, and
JALR still resolves in EX.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because no PC decision,
replay hit/miss rule, branch direction rule, direct-JAL rule, stall rule,
memory protocol, divider protocol, writeback rule, or retirement rule
changes. The intended Pareto win is a small LUT4 reduction and/or higher
median Fmax from replacing two independent 7-bit opcode compares in the IF
static predictor cone with one shared prefix check plus narrow class
selects, while preserving the already-met area target and every CLAUDE.md
invariant.

changes.description:
Add a shared decode-light predicate for the IF static predictor's
branch/direct-JAL opcode family, then derive `branch_opcode` and
`jal_opcode` from the low opcode class bits instead of using two full
7-bit equality checks. Preserve the branch funct3 legality check,
backward-branch rule, direct-JAL prediction, shared `predict_target`,
alignment checks, JALR-unpredicted behavior, `fetch_kill` gating,
redirect priority, replay lookup PC selection, live-imem priority,
replay candidate guard, fill bypass, MRU replacement, PC update
semantics, `fetch_ready`, and IF/ID payload fields.

Update only IF predictor comments if useful to document that the
decode-light static predictor shares a common opcode-family predecode
for mutually exclusive BRANCH and JAL cases. Do not change bundle
fields, control encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field widths,
top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Updated `rtl/if_stage.sv` so the IF static predictor derives BRANCH and
  direct-JAL recognition from a shared control-flow opcode-family predecode:
  `fetch_instr[6:4] == 3'b110` and `fetch_instr[1:0] == 2'b11`.
- Derived `branch_opcode` from that shared predicate plus
  `fetch_instr[3:2] == 2'b00`.
- Derived `jal_opcode` from that shared predicate plus
  `fetch_instr[3:2] == 2'b11`.
- Updated the local IF-stage comment to document the shared BRANCH/JAL
  predecode.

## Deviations

- No functional deviations from the hypothesis. I did not change
  `rtl/core_pkg.sv` because no bundle fields or control encodings needed
  documentation changes beyond the IF-stage comment.

## Concerns

- None about the RTL change. The predicate is bit-equivalent to the prior
  exact opcode checks for BRANCH (`7'b1100011`) and JAL (`7'b1101111`), and
  JALR (`7'b1100111`) remains outside both derived predicates.
- Running `bash formal/run_all.sh` directly in this worktree was blocked by
  the session sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared
  tree outside the writable worktree, and the script stages files there. To
  exercise the same gate, I copied the patched `rtl/` and formal inputs into
  `/tmp/auto_arch_formal_run.P0mok9` with a writable copy of `riscv-formal`
  and ran the same `bash formal/run_all.sh` command from that temporary tree.

## Local verification

- Lint: pass.
  - `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`
- Formal: pass.
  - In-place command was sandbox-blocked before proof generation.
  - Temporary writable copy result: `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.
- Formal fix attempts: 0; no RTL counterexample was produced.

title: Bundle-Stable RVFI Memory Data Constants
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The micro_opt slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto
free win: preserve CoreMark cycles while shaving a little MEM/WB routing
or improving median Fmax without changing packed bundle widths.

The latest accepted improvement used the safest recent pattern for this
design: keep the pipeline bundle layout stable, but drive downstream-dead
metadata to deterministic constants so synthesis can prune fanout without
the placement churn of a type-width change. The MEM/WB memory-data rails
have the same shape. `mem_wb_t.mem_rdata` is only meaningful when
`mem_rmask` is nonzero, and `mem_wb_t.mem_wdata` is only meaningful when
`mem_wmask` is nonzero. For non-loads and non-stores respectively, RVFI
ignores those bytes by mask, yet the current MEM stage still latches the
raw dmem read word and replicated store data every cycle.

This is narrower than prior MEM/WB result projection or control
projection attempts. It does not alter the writeback data path, memory
protocol, store slot, pipeline bundle field names, or any scheduling rule.
It only constantizes RVFI memory data payloads on cycles where the
corresponding RVFI mask already says no memory bytes are architecturally
observed.
hypothesis:
In `rtl/mem_stage.sv`, keep all memory access, load extraction, store data,
and RVFI mask logic exactly as today, but qualify the data payloads written
into the MEM/WB RVFI memory fields with the already-existing access masks.

On a normal MEM/WB update, latch:

`reg_q.mem_rdata <= (mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_read) ? dmem_rdata : 32'b0;`

and:

`reg_q.mem_wdata <= (mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_write) ? wdata_rep : 32'b0;`

while leaving `reg_q.mem_rmask` and `reg_q.mem_wmask` generated exactly as
today. Valid legal loads still report the raw memory word through
`mem_rdata`, including byte and halfword loads where RVFI uses the byte
mask to identify the selected lanes. Valid legal stores still report the
replicated byte-lane write data through `mem_wdata`. Non-memory
instructions, illegal instructions, misaligned memory traps, loads with no
write mask, and stores with no read mask continue to report zero masks; the
now-zero data payload is architecturally invisible under those masks.

Preserve the existing `hold_wb` behavior exactly. During a MEM wait, clear
only `valid` and retain all previous MEM/WB fields, including the
qualified `mem_rdata` or `mem_wdata` from the previously completed memory
instruction. Do not change live dmem outputs, byte/halfword write
replication, load lane muxes, misalignment traps, store-slot enqueue and
wait behavior, pending-store drain priority, writeback data selection,
forwarding, branch/JAL/JALR repair, replay prediction, divider protocol,
top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0 retirement ordering,
channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder illegal-default
discipline, or CoreMark timing markers.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because no pipeline
state, handshake rule, PC decision, memory-ordering rule, writeback rule,
or retirement rule changes. The intended Pareto win is a small LUT4
reduction and/or higher median Fmax from removing unnecessary dmem read
and store-data fanout into RVFI memory data registers on masked-off cycles,
while preserving the already-met area target and every CLAUDE.md invariant.

changes.description:
Qualify only the MEM/WB RVFI memory data payloads. Latch
`mem_rdata` from `dmem_rdata` only for valid legal aligned loads, and
otherwise latch zero. Latch `mem_wdata` from the replicated store data
only for valid legal aligned stores, and otherwise latch zero. Preserve
the existing read/write masks, aligned address reporting, load
extraction, store-data replication, misalignment traps, dmem request
gating, pending-store enqueue and wait behavior, MEM/WB hold semantics,
module ports, and all externally visible behavior for masked bytes.

Update comments only, if useful, to document that `mem_wb_t.mem_rdata`
and `mem_wb_t.mem_wdata` are bundle-stable RVFI payload rails whose
values are meaningful only under their corresponding masks. Do not
change bundle field names or widths, ALU encodings, decoder illegal
defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2
contract.

implementation_notes:
Bundle-Stable RVFI Memory Data Constants
========================================

What changed
------------
- `rtl/mem_stage.sv`: Qualified only the MEM/WB RVFI memory data payload
  flops:
  - `mem_rdata` now latches `dmem_rdata` only when the existing legal aligned
    load predicate is true: `mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_read`.
  - `mem_wdata` now latches replicated store data only when the existing legal
    aligned store predicate is true: `mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_write`.
  - Otherwise both payload rails latch `32'b0`.
- `rtl/core_pkg.sv`: Updated comments on `mem_wb_t.mem_rdata` and
  `mem_wb_t.mem_wdata` to document that their values are meaningful only under
  `mem_rmask` / `mem_wmask`.

Deviations
----------
- No RTL behavioral deviations from the hypothesis. The dmem outputs, load
  extraction, store replication, mask generation, misalignment trapping,
  store-slot behavior, and `hold_wb` semantics are unchanged.
- The first direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` invocation could not stage files
  because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared checkout outside the
  writable sandbox, so `rm -f formal/riscv-formal/cores/.../*.sv` failed with
  `Operation not permitted` before any formal checks ran. I then ran the same
  script from a writable `/tmp` staging tree with the current `rtl/` symlinked
  in and the riscv-formal check assets copied locally.

Concerns
--------
- None known. The changed data rails are architecturally ignored whenever the
  corresponding RVFI mask is zero.

Local verification
------------------
- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked before checks by sandbox write denial in
  the shared symlinked riscv-formal tree.
- Equivalent writable-staging run of `formal/run_all.sh`: pass,
  `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Bundle-Stable EX/MEM Illegal Store Payload Constant
motivation:
The current selected RTL meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19 CoreMark
iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500 target. The
structural slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto free win:
preserve CoreMark cycles while trimming routing or improving median Fmax
without changing packed bundle widths, the replay frontend, or memory
ordering.

The best recent structural result came from a bundle-stable cleanup:
keeping EX/MEM fields in place but driving downstream-dead branch metadata
to constants. A similarly narrow opportunity remains on the store-data
payload when an instruction has already been marked trapping in EX. For a
misaligned branch or jump, EX sets `ctrl.is_illegal`, clears `reg_write`,
suppresses redirect, and forces the instruction down the trap path. Such an
instruction can never perform a store, but the normal EX/MEM assignment
still carries the forwarded rs2 value on `write_data`.

A broader store-only `write_data` constantization was placement-noisy
because it qualified all non-store instructions and changed common
EX/MEM payload toggling. This hypothesis is narrower: only instructions
that EX itself has converted into traps lose the live store-data rail.
Those cases are cold in CoreMark, but the change removes unnecessary
trap-path rs2 fanout while keeping the hot legal-instruction store-data
shape untouched.
hypothesis:
Keep `ex_mem_t.write_data` and all packed bundle fields unchanged, but
constantize the store-data payload only when EX has produced a trapping
control value. In `rtl/ex_stage.sv`, after `ctrl_with_trap` has been built,
drive:

`reg_q.write_data <= ctrl_with_trap.is_illegal ? 32'b0 : rs2;`

on the normal execute path. The divide-complete path can keep its existing
deterministic zero branch metadata and existing non-store `write_data`
behavior, or drive zero for divide-class instructions if that is already
locally simpler, because divide/remainder instructions never assert
`mem_write`.

For every valid legal store, `ctrl_with_trap.is_illegal` is zero, so MEM
still receives the exact same forwarded rs2 value for byte/halfword
replication, live dmem write data, pending-store enqueue data, and RVFI
`mem_wdata`. For illegal instructions, misaligned control-flow traps, and
other trap-path payloads, MEM suppresses memory access and reports zero
masks, so the EX/MEM `write_data` value is architecturally dead and can be
a constant without affecting RVFI memory semantics. `rs2_val` remains
unchanged for RVFI source-data reporting.

This is intentionally much narrower than removing or unifying the
`write_data` field. It preserves packed struct widths, module ports,
forwarding priority, load-use timing, branch/JAL/JALR repair, replay
prediction, pending-store behavior, dmem handshakes, divider latency,
writeback timing, top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0 retirement
ordering, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder illegal-default
discipline, memory traps, and CoreMark timing markers.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because this touches
only trap-path metadata. The intended Pareto win is a small Fmax or LUT4
improvement from pruning forwarded-rs2 fanout on EX-generated trap
payloads while keeping the legal hot path and stable bundle topology
unchanged.

changes.description:
In the normal EX/MEM register update, qualify only the `write_data`
assignment with the trap-adjusted control value. Preserve the existing
forwarded `rs2` value for all non-trapping instructions, but drive
`32'b0` when `ctrl_with_trap.is_illegal` is true. Leave `rs2_val`
untouched for RVFI source reporting, and preserve local branch/jump
resolution, misaligned-target trap gating, divider launch/completion,
EX/MEM valid/stall behavior, operand forwarding, JAL/JALR link-data
routing, store-data behavior for legal stores, and all RVFI-visible
semantics.

No behavior change is intended. Audit comments only if needed to make
clear that `in.write_data` is semantically consumed only when
`mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_write` is true; trapping or illegal
instructions suppress dmem enables and RVFI masks regardless of the
dead payload value.

Update comments only if useful to document that `ex_mem_t.write_data`
is retained as a bundle-stable store-data rail and may be constant on
trap-path payloads. Do not change bundle field names or widths, ALU
encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO
assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes

- Updated `rtl/ex_stage.sv` so the normal EX/MEM register update drives
  `reg_q.write_data` from forwarded `rs2` only when the trap-adjusted EX
  control is legal:
  `ctrl_with_trap.is_illegal ? 32'b0 : rs2`.
- Left the divide-complete path unchanged; it still carries `div_rs2_q` as
  before.
- Updated comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` and `rtl/mem_stage.sv` to document
  that `write_data` remains a bundle-stable store-data rail and is only
  semantically consumed by valid, legal stores.

## Deviations

- No functional deviation from the hypothesis. Packed bundle fields, ports,
  divider behavior, MEM access gating, RVFI source-data rails, and legal
  store data behavior are unchanged.

## Concerns

- None identified. The change only affects EX-produced trap payload metadata;
  legal stores still see the exact forwarded `rs2` value.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` in the repository failed before generating checks
  because the script tried to refresh files under the staged
  `formal/riscv-formal/cores/auto-arch-researcher` tree and the sandbox
  rejected those deletes with `Operation not permitted`.
- To complete the local formal self-check, I copied the same RTL, wrapper,
  checks config, and a dereferenced writable copy of `formal/riscv-formal`
  into `/tmp` and ran the same `formal/run_all.sh` there. Result:
  `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.
- No RTL fix attempts were needed after formal; no counterexample was seen.

title: Minimal Replay-Clocked Agree BHT
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The predictor slot therefore needs a strict Pareto free win:
improve CoreMark throughput without giving back the tight LUT result or
weakening the RVFI contract.

The experiment log makes the predictor boundary clear. The two-bank
instruction replay predictor and the static backward-branch/direct-JAL
policy should stay intact; replay-table reshaping, return prediction,
tagged BHTs, and larger tagless BHTs generally spent too much Fmax or
area. The useful signal from those failed direction-predictor attempts is
that branch direction can reduce CoreMark cycles, but the implementation
must be much smaller and must avoid adding another current-PC compare or
target metadata path in IF.

A minimal agree-style overlay is the lowest-cost remaining predictor angle.
It keeps the existing static sign-bit rule as the base prediction and stores
only whether recent branches agreed with that static bias. By clocking the
counter lookup with the same lookahead PC machinery already used by the
replay predictor, the current IF decision consumes only a registered counter
bit plus the existing static branch decode. This targets the cycle gain seen
from BHT experiments while explicitly avoiding the structures that caused
their LUT/Fmax regressions: tags, valid bits, target storage, extra candidate
PC registers, and same-cycle update bypasses.
hypothesis:
Add a tiny tagless agree-BHT overlay for conditional branch direction, but
make it deliberately smaller and lower-fanout than the previous BHT
attempts. Use four 2-bit saturating counters indexed by fetch word PC bits
`[3:2]`. Reset every counter to weak-agree (`2'b10`) so cold behavior is
exactly the current static predictor. Do not add tags, valid bits, target
storage, return-stack state, predicted-target payload, or a new candidate-PC
register.

Read the counter using the same lookahead PC that already feeds the replay
candidate: EX redirect target on redirect, the IF-computed next PC on
normal advance, and the held PC on stalls. Register only the selected 2-bit
counter. In the current fetch cycle, use the registered counter only when
the existing replay lookahead PC register matches the current PC; otherwise
fall back to the static rule. This should reuse the existing
`replay_cand_pc_q == pc` equality as a shared "lookahead is for this PC"
guard instead of adding another wide comparator.

For a legal aligned conditional branch, compute the base direction from the
existing static rule (`fetch_instr[31]`). If the registered counter predicts
agree, use the base direction. If it predicts disagree, invert the base
direction. Direct JAL prediction, JALR-unpredicted behavior, the shared
static target adder, branch funct3 legality, target-alignment guard, replay
table contents, live-imem priority, fill bypass, MRU replacement, redirect
priority, `fetch_ready`, and PC update semantics remain unchanged.

Train the four counters from EX on resolved, valid, legal, non-trapping
conditional branches. The update index comes from the branch PC bits `[3:2]`.
The trained outcome is `branch_taken == in.instr[31]`, meaning the resolved
branch agreed with the static sign-bit bias. Saturating-increment on agree
and saturating-decrement on disagree. Gate the update with `!stall` so a
branch held behind a MEM wait does not update repeatedly. Do not add a
same-cycle update-to-lookup bypass; a one-iteration delay is acceptable and
keeps the IF path smaller.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should improve if even one or two hot branches are
consistently misclassified by the static backward-taken rule. LUT4 should
remain below the current target because the overlay is only eight counter
bits plus narrow update logic and reuses the replay lookahead guard. If the
counters alias badly, EX repair remains authoritative and the design falls
back to ordinary mispredictions; correctness, RVFI ordering, decoder
legality, memory behavior, divider behavior, and the top-level IO contract
are unchanged.

changes.description:
Add a four-entry tagless agree-BHT overlay for conditional branch
direction. Register the selected counter from the same lookahead PC used
by the replay predictor, and use it only when the existing replay
candidate PC guard proves the lookahead corresponds to the current fetch
PC. For legal aligned conditional branches, choose either the current
static direction or its inverse based on the registered agree counter.
Do not add tags, valid bits, target storage, a return stack, a new
candidate-PC register, or same-cycle update bypass. Preserve direct-JAL
prediction, JALR-unpredicted behavior, the shared `predict_target`,
replay table geometry, live-imem priority, fill bypass, MRU replacement,
redirect priority over stall, PC update semantics, `fetch_ready`, and
IF/ID payload fields.

Add internal BHT training outputs carrying update valid, branch PC, and
the resolved agree/disagree outcome. Assert the update only for valid,
legal, non-misaligned conditional branches, and gate it with `!stall` so
a branch held behind a MEM wait does not train repeatedly. Preserve
branch misprediction repair, jump handling, misaligned-target trap
gating, divider launch/completion, operand forwarding, EX/MEM valid and
stall behavior, JAL/JALR link-data routing, and all RVFI-visible
semantics.

Wire the new EX-to-IF agree-BHT update signals between `ex_stage` and
`if_stage` without changing the top-level `core` IO. Preserve IF replay
wiring, hazard and forwarding connections, dmem arbitration, RVFI
channel layout, channel-0 order counter, channel-1 tie-off, and all
existing retirement behavior.

Update comments only, if useful, to document that `predicted_taken` may
be the static branch direction adjusted by a tiny replay-clocked
agree-BHT overlay, while direct JAL prediction and replay-table behavior
remain unchanged. Do not change bundle fields, control encodings,
decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions,
or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
## What changed

- Added a four-entry tagless agree-BHT overlay in `rtl/if_stage.sv`.
- The counters are 2-bit saturating counters indexed by fetch word PC bits `[3:2]` and reset to weak-agree (`2'b10`).
- The BHT lookup is clocked from the same `replay_lookup_pc` path already used by the replay predictor: redirect target on redirect, predicted next PC on advance, and held PC on stall.
- The current IF decision uses the registered counter only when the existing replay candidate PC matches the current PC. Otherwise it falls back to the static sign-bit branch rule.
- Conditional branch prediction now uses the static sign-bit base direction, optionally inverted when the registered agree counter predicts disagree. Direct JAL prediction, JALR behavior, target calculation, replay table behavior, and fetch-ready behavior are otherwise unchanged.
- Added EX-stage training outputs in `rtl/ex_stage.sv`: update valid, branch PC, and agree/disagree outcome.
- Training is asserted only for valid, legal, non-misaligned conditional branches and is gated with `!stall` so a branch held behind a MEM wait does not update repeatedly.
- Wired the EX-to-IF training signals through `rtl/core.sv` without changing top-level `core` IO.
- Updated only comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to document that `predicted_taken` may include the tiny agree-BHT overlay.

## Deviations

- No functional deviation from the hypothesis.
- Added a local Verilator `UNUSEDSIGNAL` lint waiver around the unused bits of `bht_update_pc`; the BHT intentionally uses only `pc[3:2]` for its four-entry index.
- The first direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` invocation in the worktree was blocked before any proof because this sandbox disallows writes through the `formal/riscv-formal` symlink target. I copied the same RTL and formal tree to `/tmp/auto-arch-formal-run.eo4XdT` and ran the same script there.

## Concerns

- The predictor is intentionally tiny and tagless, so branch aliasing across the four entries may reduce or reverse the intended performance gain on some workloads. EX repair remains authoritative, so this is a performance risk, not a correctness risk.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked in-place by sandbox permissions before proof staging.
- `/tmp/auto-arch-formal-run.eo4XdT$ bash formal/run_all.sh`: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Bundle-Stable Dead BranchOp Constant
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The micro_opt slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto free
win: preserve CoreMark cycles while shaving a little control fanout or
improving median Fmax without changing packed bundle widths.

The most successful recent micro-optimization used a low-risk pattern for
this design: keep the pipeline bundle layout stable, but drive metadata
that is dead after EX to deterministic constants so synthesis can prune
fanout without the placement churn of changing struct widths. That worked
for the registered EX/MEM branch outcome and target rails.

A smaller member of the same dead post-EX class remains in `ctrl_t`:
`branch_op`. EX consumes `branch_op` locally to select the conditional
branch comparison. Once EX has produced `branch_taken`, redirect repair,
trap status, and `pc_next`, no downstream MEM, WB, forwarding, store-slot,
or RVFI logic reads the EX/MEM or MEM/WB copy of `branch_op`. A broader
control-flow constantization touched `is_branch`, `is_jump`, and `is_jalr`
at the same time and was placement-noisy. This hypothesis narrows the
bundle-stable constantization to only the three-bit branch comparator
selector.
hypothesis:
Keep `ctrl_t` and all packed pipeline bundle field names and widths
unchanged, but stop carrying the live branch comparator selector past the
execute stage. EX should continue using the original rich ID/EX
`in.ctrl.branch_op` exactly as today for BEQ/BNE/BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU
comparison and branch prediction repair.

When writing the EX/MEM register in the normal execute path, build a local
control copy from the existing trap-adjusted `ctrl_with_trap`, then drive
only:

`ctrl_to_mem.branch_op = BR_BEQ;`

before assigning `reg_q.ctrl`. Preserve every other field exactly as the
existing trap-adjusted control value, including `alu_op`, `alu_src`,
branch/jump class bits, AUIPC/LUI bits, memory controls, `reg_write`,
`mem_to_reg`, and `is_illegal`.

Apply the same one-field constantization on the divide-complete path before
assigning the completed EX/MEM payload. DIV/REM are not conditional
branches, and downstream stages do not consume `branch_op`, so using the
safe default `BR_BEQ` is architecturally invisible.

This is intentionally narrower than deleting the field or projecting the
whole post-EX control payload. It does not alter decoder outputs, ALU
operation selection, branch/JAL/JALR repair, replay behavior, load-use
interlocks, forwarding priority, pending-store behavior, dmem handshake
behavior, divider latency, writeback timing, top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2
ports, channel-0-only retirement, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`,
decoder illegal-default discipline, memory traps, or CoreMark timing
markers.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because no state,
latency, operand value, PC decision, memory-ordering rule, writeback rule,
or retirement rule changes. The intended Pareto win is a very small LUT4
reduction and/or median Fmax improvement from pruning the dead three-bit
branch selector rail after EX while preserving the current stable bundle
topology and all CLAUDE.md invariants.

changes.description:
Add a local EX/MEM control copy that starts from the existing
trap-adjusted control value and constantizes only `branch_op` to
`BR_BEQ` after EX has consumed it. Use that control copy for
`reg_q.ctrl` in both the normal execute path and the divide-complete
path. Preserve all local EX use of the original rich control, branch
and jump target arithmetic, misaligned-target trap gating, divider
launch/completion, operand forwarding, EX/MEM valid and stall behavior,
JAL/JALR link-data routing, store-data behavior, and all RVFI-visible
semantics.

Update comments only if useful to document that `ctrl_t` remains the
stable packed control type, while the EX/MEM copy may constantize
`branch_op` because branch comparison is consumed entirely inside EX.
Do not change bundle field names or widths, ALU encodings, decoder
illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the
NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
## Implementation Notes

### What changed

- Updated `rtl/ex_stage.sv` so the EX/MEM control payload keeps the stable
  `ctrl_t` layout but drives the post-EX `branch_op` rail to `BR_BEQ`.
- The normal execute path now writes `reg_q.ctrl <= ctrl_to_mem`, where
  `ctrl_to_mem` starts from the existing trap-adjusted `ctrl_with_trap` and
  changes only `branch_op`.
- The divide-complete path now writes `reg_q.ctrl <= div_ctrl_to_mem`, where
  `div_ctrl_to_mem` starts from `div_in_q.ctrl` and changes only `branch_op`.

### Deviations

- No `rtl/core_pkg.sv` comment update was needed; the RTL change is localized
  and leaves all bundle names, field widths, and encodings unchanged.
- No decoder, ALU, branch-resolution, memory, forwarding, RVFI, or top-level IO
  behavior was changed.

### Concerns

- No architectural concern found. `branch_op` is still consumed from the rich
  ID/EX control value inside EX before the EX/MEM copy is constantized.
- Direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` from the repository did not reach SBY checks
  because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared tree outside this
  sandbox, and the script failed while clearing stale staged `.sv` files with
  `Operation not permitted`.

### Local verification

- Lint: pass
  - `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`
- Formal: pass
  - To avoid the repository staging permission failure above, I ran the same
    formal flow from a dereferenced temporary copy under `/tmp`, with a fresh
    core staging name and no stale staging files to delete.
  - Result: `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Bundle-Stable MEM/WB RD Derivation
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The structural slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto
free win: preserve CoreMark cycles while shaving a little final-stage
routing or LUT4 without changing packed bundle widths, memory ordering, or
the RVFI contract.

The most useful recent structural pattern was bundle-stable metadata
pruning: keep the pipeline field layout intact, but stop carrying live
values on rails that are already recoverable or downstream-dead. The final
MEM/WB destination register rail has that shape. `mem_wb_t.rd` is always
just `mem_wb_t.instr[11:7]`, and the full instruction word is already
carried to retirement for RVFI `insn`, source-address reporting, and trap
context.

A previous all-stage destination-register derivation changed ID/EX,
EX/MEM, MEM/WB, hazard, forwarding, WB, and RVFI fanout together and was
placement-noisy. This hypothesis keeps the earlier-stage destination rails
intact for load-use detection and EX/MEM forwarding, and only removes the
duplicated destination payload at the final MEM/WB boundary. That is the
narrowest version of the same structural cleanup.
hypothesis:
Keep the `mem_wb_t.rd` field and all packed bundle widths unchanged, but
make the field a bundle-stability placeholder after MEM. In
`rtl/mem_stage.sv`, continue consuming `in.rd` exactly as today for any
EX/MEM-local behavior, but latch the MEM/WB copy as a deterministic
constant, for example:

`reg_q.rd <= 5'b0;`

Then derive every final-stage destination use from the carried instruction
word instead of from `mem_wb_w.rd`. In `wb_stage.sv`, drive the register
file write address from `in.instr[11:7]`, still gated by
`in.ctrl.reg_write && in.valid`. In `core.sv`, feed the MEM/WB forwarding
destination and RVFI `rd_addr` predicate from `mem_wb_w.instr[11:7]`.
The regfile x0 guard remains unchanged, and RVFI still reports
`rd_addr = 0` / `rd_wdata = 0` whenever the existing writeback predicate is
false.

This does not touch the ID/EX or EX/MEM `rd` rails. The load-use
interlock still sees the ID/EX destination exactly as today, and EX/MEM
forwarding still compares against the existing EX/MEM destination. During
a MEM wait, the existing `hold_wb` behavior continues to clear only
`valid`; the held producer's destination is now retained by the held
instruction word instead of a separate `rd` field, so MEM/WB forwarding
remains available through the existing `wb_w_data` path.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because no PC decision,
operand value, branch/JAL/JALR repair, load-use timing, forwarding
priority, divider protocol, pending-store behavior, dmem handshake,
writeback timing, or retirement rule changes. The intended Pareto win is a
small LUT4 and routing reduction from pruning the duplicated final-stage
destination-register payload while preserving the stable MEM/WB bundle
shape, top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only retirement,
channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder illegal-default
discipline, memory traps, and CoreMark timing markers.

changes.description:
Stop latching a live destination-register value into the MEM/WB `rd`
field. On normal MEM/WB updates, drive `reg_q.rd` to a deterministic
constant because all final-stage consumers will derive the destination
from `reg_q.instr[11:7]`. Preserve load extraction, byte/halfword
masks, misalignment traps, dmem request gating, store-slot enqueue and
wait behavior, MEM/WB hold semantics, RVFI memory payload fields,
module ports, and all externally visible behavior.

Drive `w_addr` from `in.instr[11:7]` instead of `in.rd`. Preserve the
write-enable condition, write-data mux, register-file x0 behavior, and
RVFI `rd_wdata` semantics through the existing top-level `wb_w_data`
path. Update comments so `mem_wb_t.rd` is no longer described as a
semantically live WB input.

Derive the MEM/WB forwarding destination and RVFI destination reporting
from `mem_wb_w.instr[11:7]`. Keep EX/MEM forwarding, ID/EX load-use
detection, WB data forwarding, RVFI field names and widths, channel-0
order counter, channel-1 tie-off, IF replay wiring, dmem arbitration,
and the `core` module IO unchanged.

Update comments only to document that the MEM/WB `rd` field is retained
for bundle-shape stability while final writeback, forwarding, and RVFI
destination addresses are derived from the carried instruction word. Do
not change bundle field names or widths, ALU encodings, decoder illegal
defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2
contract.

implementation_notes:
## Implementation Notes

### What changed

- `rtl/mem_stage.sv`: MEM/WB still keeps the `rd` field in `mem_wb_t`, but normal MEM/WB updates now latch `reg_q.rd <= 5'b0`. The held MEM/WB fields used during `hold_wb` are still retained; forwarding now relies on the held instruction word for the destination register.
- `rtl/wb_stage.sv`: the register-file write address is derived from `in.instr[11:7]` instead of `in.rd`. The write enable and write-data mux are unchanged.
- `rtl/core.sv`: added a local `mem_wb_rd = mem_wb_w.instr[11:7]` derivation and used it for MEM/WB forwarding and RVFI channel-0 destination reporting. EX/MEM forwarding and ID/EX load-use detection still use their existing `rd` rails.
- `rtl/core_pkg.sv`: updated the MEM/WB bundle comment to document that `rd` is retained only for packed bundle shape stability.

### Deviations

- No functional deviation from the hypothesis. The only operational workaround was for local formal execution: `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to the shared contract tree, and direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` failed while trying to remove staged `.sv` files there with `Operation not permitted`. I ran the same script flow from a dereferenced `/tmp` copy, with only the temporary core staging name changed to avoid writing into the protected shared path.

### Concerns

- None from the RTL change. `mem_wb_t.rd` is now intentionally non-semantic after MEM, so any future final-stage consumer must derive the destination from `instr[11:7]`.

### Local verification

- Lint: `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv` passed.
- Formal: direct worktree invocation was blocked by sandbox/path permissions before any proof check ran. Dereferenced `/tmp` formal run passed with `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.
- Formal fix attempts: 0; there was no RTL counterexample to fix.

title: Funct3-Aware Static Branch Bias
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The predictor slot therefore needs a strict Pareto free win:
improve CoreMark throughput without disturbing the replay frontend or
giving back the tight LUT result.

The experiment log makes the boundary clear. The two-bank instruction
replay predictor and aligned direct-JAL prediction should stay intact;
replay-table reshaping, BHTs, return prediction, target metadata, and
replacement-policy changes generally spent too much Fmax or area. The
remaining promising predictor angle is a zero-state static direction
refinement for conditional branches.

The current branch policy is pure BTFNT: every legal backward conditional
branch predicts taken, and every legal forward conditional branch predicts
not taken. CoreMark's compiled branch mix commonly uses backward BEQ as a
loop-exit/rare condition and forward BNE as a skip-over-fallback pattern.
Those patterns are exactly where pure sign-bit prediction leaves cycles on
the table, while a tiny funct3-aware bias can capture much of the benefit
without adding a table, tags, update wires, target storage, or rollback
state.
hypothesis:
Change only the IF-stage static conditional-branch direction rule. Keep the
existing branch opcode check, branch funct3 legality guard, shared
`predict_target` adder, target-alignment check, direct-JAL prediction,
JALR-unpredicted behavior, replay table, live-imem priority, fill bypass,
MRU replacement, candidate-PC guard, redirect priority, `fetch_ready`, and
PC update semantics unchanged.

For legal conditional branches, replace the current direction subterm
`fetch_instr[31]` with a funct3-aware static bias:

- BEQ predicts not taken, independent of offset sign.
- BNE predicts taken, independent of offset sign.
- BLT, BGE, BLTU, and BGEU keep the current backward-taken /
  forward-not-taken sign-bit policy.

This can be implemented with a very small expression over the branch funct3
bits rather than full compares. With funct3 bits `fetch_instr[14:12]`,
reserved values 010 and 011 are already excluded by `branch_funct_legal`, so
the prediction bit can be:

`branch_static_taken = fetch_instr[14] ? fetch_instr[31] : fetch_instr[12];`

For funct3 000 (BEQ), this is zero. For funct3 001 (BNE), this is one. For
funct3 100 through 111, this falls back to the branch immediate sign bit.
The reserved funct3 values may produce either value internally, but the
existing legality guard prevents them from being predicted and they still
trap through the normal decoder/RVFI path.

EX repair remains the existing outcome-only comparison between the resolved
branch outcome and `in.predicted_taken`, so any static-policy mistake is an
ordinary misprediction and cannot affect architectural correctness. Direct
JAL behavior, JALR behavior, replay behavior, memory ordering, divider
behavior, RVFI ordering, and top-level IO are unchanged.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should improve by removing many false static
predictions on BEQ loop-exit branches and forward BNE branches. The added
hardware is only a 2:1 mux in the IF branch direction predicate, so LUT4 and
median Fmax should stay flat enough for a phase-2 Pareto win.

changes.description:
Add a local `branch_static_taken` signal for the static conditional
branch predictor. Compute it as `fetch_instr[14] ? fetch_instr[31] :
fetch_instr[12]`, relying on the existing `branch_funct_legal` guard to
exclude reserved funct3 values 010 and 011. Replace the current
`fetch_instr[31]` direction term in `branch_predict_taken` with this
signal. Preserve the branch opcode gate, branch funct3 legality check,
target-alignment guard, shared `predict_target` adder, direct-JAL
prediction, JALR-unpredicted behavior, `fetch_kill` gating, redirect
priority, replay lookup PC selection, live-imem priority, replay
candidate guard, fill bypass, MRU replacement, PC update semantics,
`fetch_ready`, and IF/ID payload fields.

Update comments only, if useful, to document that `predicted_taken` for
conditional branches comes from a funct3-aware static bias: BEQ
not-taken, BNE taken, and the signed/unsigned compare branches using the
existing backward-taken sign-bit rule. Do not change bundle fields,
control encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field widths,
top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Updated `rtl/if_stage.sv` to add `branch_static_taken`.
- Conditional branch prediction now uses:
  - BEQ: not taken
  - BNE: taken
  - BLT/BGE/BLTU/BGEU: existing backward-taken/forward-not-taken sign-bit rule
- Left the existing branch opcode check, funct3 legality guard, target alignment guard, shared `predict_target` adder, direct-JAL prediction, JALR behavior, replay table logic, redirect priority, `fetch_ready`, and PC update semantics unchanged.
- Updated the `predicted_taken` comment in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to document the funct3-aware static bias.

## Deviations

- Directly running `bash formal/run_all.sh` in the worktree failed before any checks launched because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a read-only checkout outside the writable sandbox, and the script's staging cleanup could not remove files under `formal/riscv-formal/cores/auto-arch-researcher`.

## Concerns

## Local verification status

title: Constant Divide Store-Data Rail
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The micro_opt slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto free
win: preserve CoreMark cycles while shaving a little routing or improving
median Fmax without changing packed bundle widths.

The latest accepted improvement used the lowest-risk pattern seen in this
design: keep the bundle layout stable, but drive downstream-dead payload
rails to deterministic constants after the stage that actually consumes the
information. That worked for EX/MEM branch metadata. A smaller instance of
the same pattern remains on the divide-complete path in EX.

When a DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU finishes, `ex_stage.sv` still writes the saved
forwarded rs2 value into `ex_mem_t.write_data`. That rail is only consumed
by MEM for stores. Divide and remainder instructions are non-memory ALU
writeback operations, and the saved rs2 value is already carried separately
in `rs2_val` for RVFI source-data reporting. The divide-complete
`write_data` assignment therefore adds a 32-bit input to the EX/MEM
store-data mux for no downstream semantic use.
hypothesis:
Keep `ex_mem_t.write_data` and all packed bundle fields unchanged, but
drive the divide-complete copy of `write_data` to a deterministic zero.
In `rtl/ex_stage.sv`, replace the divide-complete assignment:

`reg_q.write_data <= div_rs2_q;`

with:

`reg_q.write_data <= 32'b0;`

Preserve `reg_q.rs2_val <= div_rs2_q`, because RVFI source reporting and
any source-value checks still need the post-forwarded divide rs2 operand.
Preserve the normal EX path exactly: legal stores must continue to carry
forwarded `rs2` on `write_data` for byte/halfword replication, live dmem
write data, pending-store enqueue payloads, and RVFI `mem_wdata`.

This is intentionally narrower than store-qualifying the whole EX/MEM
`write_data` rail. It touches only the cold long-operation completion arm
and avoids adding a new predicate on the hot normal execute path. DIV/REM
result bits, divide latency, EX long-busy behavior, MEM wait behavior,
forwarding, branch/JAL/JALR repair, replay prediction, store-slot behavior,
writeback timing, top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0 retirement
ordering, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder legality, memory
traps, and CoreMark timing markers remain unchanged.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should be identical. The intended Pareto win is
a small LUT4 reduction and/or higher median Fmax from removing a dead
32-bit divide-complete input from the EX/MEM store-data register mux while
preserving the stable bundle topology and every CLAUDE.md invariant.

changes.description:
Constantize only the divide-complete EX/MEM `write_data` payload.
Drive `reg_q.write_data` to `32'b0` when a completed DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU
enters EX/MEM, while keeping `reg_q.rs2_val` driven by `div_rs2_q` for
RVFI source reporting. Preserve the normal execute-path store-data
assignment, divider launch/completion protocol, EX/MEM stall behavior,
branch and jump target handling, misaligned-target trap gating, operand
forwarding, JAL/JALR link-data routing, and all RVFI-visible semantics.

Update comments only if useful to document that `ex_mem_t.write_data`
is a store-data rail and may be constant on non-store long-operation
completion paths, while `rs2_val` remains the RVFI/source-value rail.
Do not change bundle field names or widths, ALU encodings, decoder
illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the
NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
## Constant Divide Store-Data Rail

### What changed

- In `rtl/ex_stage.sv`, the divide/remainder completion arm now drives
  `reg_q.write_data <= 32'b0` instead of copying `div_rs2_q`.
- The normal one-cycle EX path is unchanged and still drives
  `reg_q.write_data <= rs2`, preserving store data forwarding into MEM.
- `reg_q.rs2_val` on divide completion still uses `div_rs2_q`, preserving
  RVFI/source-value reporting.
- In `rtl/core_pkg.sv`, updated the `ex_mem_t.write_data` comment to describe
  it as the store-data rail. No bundle fields, names, or widths changed.

### Deviations

- No functional deviations from the hypothesis.
- The direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` invocation in this sandbox failed before
  running proofs because its staging cleanup under `formal/riscv-formal/...`
  was denied with `Operation not permitted`. I reran the same staging and
  generated-check flow from a neutral writable temporary path and tallied the
