### Concerns

- None identified. The changed `write_data` value is only on the non-memory
  divide/remainder completion path; `rs2_val` remains live for RVFI.

### Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- Formal generated-check flow equivalent to `bash formal/run_all.sh`: pass,
  `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Bundle-Stable Nonwriter RD Constants
motivation:
The current selected RTL meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19 CoreMark
iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500 target.
The structural slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto free win:
preserve CoreMark cycles while shaving a little routing or improving
median Fmax without changing packed bundle widths, memory ordering, or
the RVFI contract.

The best recent structural result used a low-risk pattern for this
design: keep pipeline fields in place, but drive values that are
semantically dead at a stage boundary to deterministic constants. That
worked for the registered EX/MEM branch metadata. A similar duplicated
payload remains on the destination-register rails. For branches, stores,
fences, EBREAK, illegal instructions, and trap-adjusted control-flow or
memory faults, the carried `rd` bits are not architecturally live because
`reg_write` is false and RVFI reports no destination register write.

Today those dead `rd` bits still come from raw instruction fields and
flow through the hazard, forwarding, WB, and RVFI-adjacent pipeline
region, even though every real consumer is gated by the corresponding
write-enable. A previous attempt removed destination fields across
multiple stages and changed fanout shape broadly; this hypothesis keeps
every bundle field and module interface stable, and only constantizes
the destination value when the instruction is known not to write a
register.
hypothesis:
Keep the `rd` members in `id_ex_t`, `ex_mem_t`, and `mem_wb_t`, but make
them nonzero-write payloads rather than raw instruction copies.

In ID, latch `reg_q.rd` from `in.instr[11:7]` only when the decoded
control says the instruction intends to write a register; otherwise latch
`5'b0`. This keeps load-use behavior unchanged because every load still
has `mem_read=1` and `reg_write=1`, while branches, stores, fences,
EBREAK, and illegal instructions no longer carry irrelevant immediate
bits on the ID/EX destination rail.

In EX, after the existing misaligned control-flow trap adjustment, latch
`reg_q.rd` from the incoming destination only when the trap-adjusted
`ctrl_with_trap.reg_write` remains true; otherwise latch `5'b0`. This
preserves all legal writers and clears the destination for trapped JAL or
JALR instructions whose register write has been suppressed. Apply the
same rule to the divide-complete path using the saved divide control.

In MEM, after the existing memory-misalignment trap adjustment, latch
`reg_q.rd` from `in.rd` only when the trap-adjusted control still has
`reg_write`; otherwise latch `5'b0`. The existing `hold_wb` behavior must
remain exact: during a MEM wait, clear only `valid` and retain the held
producer's destination and data for MEM/WB forwarding.

This should be architecturally invisible. WB still writes only when
`ctrl.reg_write && valid`; the register file still drops x0 writes; RVFI
still reports `rd_addr=0` and `rd_wdata=0` whenever the existing `rd_wen`
predicate is false; and true register writers still carry the same
destination value as today. No PC decision, operand value, branch/JAL/JALR
repair, load-use timing, forwarding priority, divider protocol,
pending-store behavior, dmem handshake, writeback timing, or retirement
rule changes.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical. The intended Pareto
win is a small LUT4 reduction and/or median Fmax improvement from
removing dead raw-instruction destination-bit fanout on nonwriters and
trap-suppressed writers while preserving the current stable bundle shape,
top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0-only retirement, channel-1
tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder illegal-default discipline, memory
traps, and CoreMark timing markers.

changes.description:
Keep the ID/EX `rd` field, but latch `in.instr[11:7]` only when the
decoded pipeline control has `reg_write` asserted; otherwise latch
`5'b0`. Preserve decode outputs, immediate generation, register-file
read addresses, source data capture, prediction metadata, stall/flush
behavior, all other control fields, and valid propagation.

Qualify the EX/MEM destination payload with the trap-adjusted
register-write bit. In the normal execute path, assign `reg_q.rd`
from `in.rd` only when `ctrl_with_trap.reg_write` is true; otherwise
assign `5'b0`. Apply the same nonwriter-zero rule on the
divide-complete path from the saved divide control. Preserve branch
and jump target arithmetic, misaligned-target trap gating, divider
launch/completion, operand forwarding, EX/MEM valid and stall
behavior, JAL/JALR link-data routing, store-data behavior, and all
RVFI-visible semantics.

Qualify the MEM/WB destination payload with the memory-trap-adjusted
register-write bit. On normal MEM/WB updates, latch `in.rd` only when
the final control still has `reg_write`; otherwise latch `5'b0`.
Preserve load extraction, byte/halfword masks, misalignment traps,
dmem request gating, store-slot enqueue and wait behavior, MEM/WB
hold semantics, RVFI memory payload fields, module ports, and all
externally visible behavior.

Update comments only to document that the carried `rd` fields remain
in the stable packed bundle layout but may be constant zero for
non-writing or trap-suppressed instructions. Do not change bundle
field names or widths, ALU encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI
field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes Made

- `rtl/id_stage.sv`: ID/EX `rd` now latches `instr[11:7]` only when decoded `reg_write` is asserted; otherwise it latches `5'b0`.
- `rtl/ex_stage.sv`: EX/MEM `rd` now latches the incoming destination only when the trap-adjusted control still has `reg_write` asserted. The divide-complete path applies the same rule using the saved divide control.
- `rtl/mem_stage.sv`: MEM/WB `rd` now latches the incoming destination only when the memory-trap-adjusted control still has `reg_write` asserted. `hold_wb` behavior is unchanged and still clears only `valid`.
- `rtl/core_pkg.sv`: comments now document that the stable `rd` bundle fields may carry zero for nonwriters or trap-suppressed writers.

## Deviations

- No RTL deviations from the hypothesis plan.
- Verification deviation: running `bash formal/run_all.sh` directly in the worktree failed before generating checks because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared checkout outside the writable sandbox, and the script attempts to remove staged `.sv` files there. I reran the same script from a dereferenced temporary copy under `/tmp` so the staged riscv-formal tree was writable.

## Concerns

- No known RTL concerns. The bundle shapes, port lists, control fields, forwarding gates, and MEM/WB hold semantics are unchanged.

## Local Verification

- Lint: `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv` passed.
- Formal: direct worktree invocation was sandbox-blocked before RTL checks. Equivalent dereferenced temporary invocation passed with `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.
- Fix attempts: 0 RTL fix attempts were needed; there was no formal counterexample.

title: Reset-Decoupled Static Predictor Enable
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The predictor slot therefore needs a strict Pareto free win:
preserve CoreMark cycles while shaving a little IF predictor control
fanout or improving median Fmax.

The experiment log makes the predictor boundary clear. The two-bank
replay predictor and the existing static backward-branch/direct-JAL policy
should stay intact; replay-table reshaping, BHTs, return prediction,
target metadata, direct mapping, tag compression, and replacement-policy
changes have generally spent more Fmax or area than they recovered. The
only successful predictor-family changes have been tiny timing cleanups
that did not change the instruction stream.

The remaining low-risk cleanup is inside the static predictor enable.
`if_stage.sv` currently derives `predict_enable` from `fetch_kill`, so the
branch and JAL prediction predicates depend on reset, flush, and redirect.
Flush and redirect are runtime controls that must still suppress ignored
IF payloads. Reset, however, is not semantically needed in the predictor
direction cone: the PC register is synchronously forced to RESET_PC, the
replay candidate valid bit and bank valid bits are reset, and the IF/ID
payload is already invalidated through `fetch_kill`.
hypothesis:
Keep `fetch_kill = reset || flush || redirect` exactly as today for the
architecturally meaningful IF/ID outputs: NOP selection, `out.valid`, and
any comments or payload-valid reasoning. Change only the local static
predictor enable so it is killed by `flush` and `redirect`, but not by
`reset`, for example:

`predict_enable = !(flush || redirect);`

The branch and direct-JAL predicates remain otherwise unchanged: BRANCH
opcode plus legal funct3, backward branch immediate sign, shared
`predict_target`, target-alignment check, direct-JAL opcode and alignment,
and JALR-unpredicted behavior all stay the same. Redirect still has top
priority in `next_pc` and in the PC register update; reset still has top
priority in the PC register. The replay lookup, fill, MRU replacement,
live-imem priority, same-entry fill bypass, full candidate-PC guard, and
`fetch_ready` semantics remain unchanged.

During reset, `predicted_taken` may be a don't-care function of the ignored
fetch word, but `out.valid` is zero and `out.instr` is NOP because
`fetch_kill` still includes reset. The reset branch of the sequential
replay block also prevents any predictor table allocation or candidate use
from that value. After reset deasserts, every runtime PC decision and replay
hit/miss behavior is bit-identical to the current design.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because the timed
instruction stream, replay behavior, branch direction rule, direct-JAL
rule, JALR policy, stall rule, memory protocol, divider protocol,
writeback rule, and retirement rule do not change. The intended Pareto win
is a small LUT4 and/or median Fmax improvement by removing high-fanout
reset from the IF static branch/JAL prediction predicate cone while
preserving the already-met LUT target and every CLAUDE.md invariant.

changes.description:
Decouple reset from the static predictor enable only. Keep
`fetch_kill` unchanged for invalidating the IF/ID payload, but change
`predict_enable` so branch and direct-JAL prediction are suppressed by
`flush` or `redirect`, not by `reset`. Preserve the branch opcode and
funct3 legality checks, backward-branch rule, direct-JAL prediction,
shared `predict_target` adder, target-alignment checks,
JALR-unpredicted behavior, redirect priority, PC register reset and
write-enable behavior, replay lookup PC selection, replay bank
structure, live-imem priority, fill bypass, MRU replacement,
candidate-PC guard, `fetch_ready`, and IF/ID output semantics.

Update comments only if useful to clarify that prediction metadata is
meaningful only when the IF payload is valid; reset can leave the local
static predictor outcome as a don't-care while `fetch_kill` still
invalidates the payload. Do not change bundle fields, control
encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO
assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Updated `rtl/if_stage.sv` so `fetch_kill` remains `reset || flush || redirect` for IF/ID invalidation, while the local static predictor enable is now `!(flush || redirect)`.
- Left the branch opcode check, branch funct3 legality check, backward-branch rule, direct-JAL rule, shared target adder, alignment checks, JALR behavior, redirect priority, PC reset behavior, replay table, and fetch-ready semantics unchanged.
- Updated the `if_id_t.predicted_taken` comment in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to state that prediction metadata is meaningful only when `valid=1`; during reset the local predictor outcome may be don't-care while `fetch_kill` still forces `valid=0` and `instr=NOP`.

## Deviations

- No RTL behavior deviations from the hypothesis. The only extra change is a comment clarification in `rtl/core_pkg.sv`.

## Concerns

- No known implementation concerns. Runtime prediction suppression still depends on `flush` and `redirect`; reset still invalidates the IF payload and resets the PC and replay-valid state.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- Direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` from the worktree was blocked before any checks ran because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared checkout outside the writable sandbox, and the script stages RTL by removing files under that target.
- Equivalent local formal run from a temporary copy under `/tmp/autoarch-formal.ApfwY9`: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: WB-Qualified RVFI RD Predicate
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The micro_opt slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto
free win: preserve CoreMark cycles while shaving a little final-stage
control fanout or improving median Fmax.

Recent successful changes have been very narrow bundle-stable cleanups.
Larger rewrites in the predictor, MEM/WB payloads, control projection,
register file, ALU, and divider have been placement-sensitive. A small
remaining duplication is in the RVFI destination writeback predicate in
`core.sv`: `rd_wen` recomputes `mem_wb_w.ctrl.reg_write && rd != 0`
even though the WB stage already produces a valid-qualified write enable
and destination address for the register file.

Reusing the existing WB-stage writeback signals for RVFI destination
reporting should reduce one late top-level control predicate and remove
stale destination/data toggling on MEM/WB invalid hold cycles, without
changing any architecturally visible valid retirement.
hypothesis:
In `rtl/core.sv`, derive the RVFI channel-0 destination write predicate
from the already existing WB-stage outputs:

`rd_wen = wb_w_en && (wb_w_addr != 5'b0);`

Then use `wb_w_addr` for `io_rvfi_rd_addr_0` when `rd_wen` is true, and
keep using `wb_w_data` for `io_rvfi_rd_wdata_0`. For every valid retiring
instruction this is bit-equivalent to the current behavior: WB asserts
`wb_w_en` exactly when `mem_wb_w.valid && mem_wb_w.ctrl.reg_write`, and
`wb_w_addr` is the MEM/WB destination register. x0 writes still report
no architectural destination because of the explicit nonzero test, and
the register file keeps its own x0 guard unchanged.

This change intentionally must not alter MEM/WB forwarding. The
forwarding unit should continue to receive `mem_wb_w.ctrl.reg_write`
rather than `wb_w_en`, because the existing MEM-wait protocol clears
`mem_wb_w.valid` while retaining the previous producer's data/control for
forwarding to a held dependent instruction. The proposed rewrite is only
for RVFI destination reporting, where `io_rvfi_valid_0` already gates
architectural visibility.

During invalid MEM/WB cycles, the new predicate drives RVFI `rd_addr` and
`rd_wdata` to zero instead of possibly exposing retained writeback fields
under `io_rvfi_valid_0 = 0`. RVFI ignores channel fields when valid is
low, so this is architecturally invisible and should only reduce
unnecessary top-level output/fanout activity.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because no pipeline
state, PC decision, hazard interlock, forwarding priority, memory
protocol, divider protocol, writeback timing, or retirement-valid rule
changes. The intended Pareto win is a small LUT4 reduction and/or higher
median Fmax from reusing the existing WB predicate in the RVFI retirement
cone while preserving the stable bundle shape, top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2
ports, channel-0-only retirement, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`,
decoder illegal-default discipline, memory traps, and CoreMark timing
markers.

changes.description:
Change only the RVFI destination writeback predicate. Derive `rd_wen`
from the existing WB-stage outputs as `wb_w_en && (wb_w_addr != 5'b0)`,
and use `wb_w_addr` for `io_rvfi_rd_addr_0` when `rd_wen` is true.
Keep `wb_w_data` as the RVFI write-data source. Do not change the
forwarding-unit inputs, especially the MEM/WB forwarding enable that
intentionally ignores `valid` during MEM waits. Preserve the RVFI
order counter, valid/trap fields, memory fields, channel-1 tie-off,
IF replay wiring, dmem arbitration, hazard and forwarding connections,
core module IO, and all retirement behavior.

Update comments only if useful to make explicit that `w_en`, `w_addr`,
and `w_data` are the canonical valid-qualified writeback signals and
may be reused by RVFI destination reporting. Do not change the module
interface, write-enable condition, result mux, destination output, or
register-file x0 behavior.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Updated `rtl/core.sv` so RVFI channel 0 derives its destination write predicate from the WB-stage outputs:
  - `rd_wen = wb_w_en && (wb_w_addr != 5'b0);`
  - `io_rvfi_rd_addr_0` now uses `wb_w_addr` when `rd_wen` is true.
- Kept `io_rvfi_rd_wdata_0` sourced from `wb_w_data`.
- Left MEM/WB forwarding unchanged: `forward_unit.mem_wb_w_en` still receives `mem_wb_w.ctrl.reg_write`.

## Deviations

- No functional deviation from the hypothesis.
- Did not change `rtl/wb_stage.sv`; the existing implementation already exposes `w_en`, `w_addr`, and `w_data` as the canonical WB outputs.

## Concerns

- No RTL concerns. This is limited to RVFI destination reporting and does not alter pipeline state, forwarding, retirement valid, or register-file write behavior.
- Running `bash formal/run_all.sh` in place failed before any RTL check because the local sandbox rejected writes/removes in the existing riscv-formal staging directory. To verify the same gate, I made a dereferenced temporary copy of `rtl/` and `formal/` under `/tmp`, changed only the temporary staging core name to avoid the protected shared path, and ran the copied `formal/run_all.sh` there.

## Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: in-place run blocked by staging-directory write permissions before checks.
- Temporary dereferenced formal run from `/tmp`: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Narrow EX/MEM Long-Op Bubble Clear
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The structural slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto
free win: preserve CoreMark cycles while shaving a little muxing, routing,
or median Fmax pressure without changing packed bundle widths, the replay
frontend, store buffering, memory ordering, or the RVFI contract.

The most reliable recent structural pattern was bundle-stable cleanup:
keep the stage payload shape intact, but stop driving live values onto
rails that are not semantically consumed. The current EX/MEM long-operation
bubble still uses a broad `reg_q <= '0` assignment while DIV/REM occupies
EX. That creates a zero-input mux on every EX/MEM payload bit, including
PC, ALU result, source values, store data, memory metadata, instruction,
and dead branch metadata, even though an invalid bubble only needs safe
control.

A prior broad valid-guarded reset pruning touched ID/EX, EX/MEM, and
MEM/WB reset/flush behavior and was placement-noisy. This hypothesis is
narrower: leave reset behavior, valid-instruction captures, MEM/WB hold
semantics, and all bundle widths unchanged, and only replace the
divider-busy EX/MEM bubble with a control-safe invalid bubble.
hypothesis:
In `rtl/ex_stage.sv`, replace only the `div_start || div_active_q`
EX/MEM bubble assignment. Instead of assigning the full `ex_mem_t` register
to zero, clear just the fields that can influence forwarding, MEM access,
writeback, or retirement when the stage is invalid:

- `reg_q.valid <= 1'b0`
- `reg_q.ctrl <= '0`
- `reg_q.rd <= 5'b0`
- keep bundle-stable dead branch metadata deterministic with
  `branch_taken <= 1'b0` and `branch_target <= 32'b0`

Leave wide data payloads such as `pc`, `alu_result`, `write_data`,
`rs1_val`, `rs2_val`, `pc_next`, and `instr` unchanged during the
divider-busy bubble. They are not architecturally observable because the
cleared control disables memory access, forwarding, writeback, and RVFI
retirement, and MEM/WB will receive `valid=0`.

Preserve the full reset behavior for EX/MEM. Preserve the normal execute
path, divide-complete path, EX/MEM stall hold on MEM wait, divider launch
and result-capture protocol, branch/JAL/JALR repair, misaligned-target
trap handling, operand forwarding, store-data behavior for legal stores,
and all RVFI-visible fields for valid instructions.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because no scheduling,
long-operation latency, PC decision, memory handshake, writeback rule, or
retirement rule changes. The intended Pareto win is a small LUT4 reduction
and/or higher median Fmax from removing a full-width zeroing mux on the
EX/MEM register during long-operation bubbles while preserving the stable
pipeline topology and every CLAUDE.md invariant.

changes.description:
Replace the full-struct `reg_q <= '0` assignment in the
`div_start || div_active_q` bubble path with explicit control-safe
clearing. Clear `valid`, `ctrl`, and `rd`, keep branch metadata
constants, and leave wide payload fields unchanged. Do not alter reset,
normal EX capture, divide completion, EX/MEM stall hold, divider
protocol, forwarding, branch/jump repair, memory behavior, or
RVFI-visible semantics for valid instructions.

Update comments only if useful to document that EX/MEM data payload
fields may retain old values during invalid long-operation bubbles,
while `valid`, `ctrl`, and `rd` are cleared so no downstream consumer
treats the bubble as a memory access, writer, or retirement. Do not
change bundle field names or widths, ALU encodings, decoder illegal
defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2
contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- In `rtl/ex_stage.sv`, replaced the divider-busy EX/MEM full-struct zeroing assignment with explicit control-safe bubble updates:
  - `reg_q.valid <= 1'b0`
  - `reg_q.ctrl <= '0`
  - `reg_q.rd <= 5'b0`
  - `reg_q.branch_taken <= 1'b0`
  - `reg_q.branch_target <= 32'b0`
- Left the wide EX/MEM payload rails unchanged during `div_start || div_active_q` bubbles, including `pc`, `alu_result`, `write_data`, `rs1_val`, `rs2_val`, `pc_next`, and `instr`.
- Added a comment in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` documenting that invalid long-operation bubbles may retain old wide payload values while `valid`, `ctrl`, and `rd` are cleared.

## Deviations

- No RTL behavior deviations from the hypothesis plan.
- Directly running `bash formal/run_all.sh` in the worktree was blocked by the sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared tree outside the writable root, and the script stages RTL by deleting/copying files there. To avoid touching the repository `formal/` tree, I copied `rtl/` and `formal/` into `/tmp/autoarch-formal-hyp-20260429-071` and ran the same unmodified `formal/run_all.sh` from that writable mirror.

## Concerns

- No known implementation concerns. The cleared control, destination register, and valid bit disable downstream memory access, forwarding, writeback, and RVFI retirement for the invalid bubble.

## Local verification status

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: pass in writable temporary mirror, final tally `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Vectorized Replay Valid Banks
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The predictor slot therefore needs a strict Pareto free win:
preserve the successful static branch/direct-JAL predictor and two-bank
replay frontend while shaving a little predictor-local control logic or
improving median Fmax.

The experiment log makes the predictor boundary clear. The two-bank
instruction replay predictor should stay intact; removing it, retiming it,
shrinking tags, direct-mapping the banks, changing replacement, and adding
branch or return predictors generally regressed either hit behavior, LUT4,
or placement. The lowest-risk remaining predictor angle is therefore a
storage-shape cleanup that leaves replay policy and timing exactly
unchanged.

The replay table currently stores each bank's valid bits as unpacked
one-bit arrays. Those valid bits are reset together, indexed in the fill
path, and indexed again in the lookahead lookup path. Packing only the two
valid-bit banks into vectors should let synthesis treat reset and indexed
selection as narrow vector operations, while leaving the resetless tag and
instruction payload arrays, per-index MRU state, and all predictor policy
untouched. This is deliberately narrower than the prior packed-metadata
attempt, which also repacked MRU and changed more of the fill/update shape.
hypothesis:
Replace the IF replay predictor's two unpacked valid-bit arrays with packed
vectors:

`logic [REPLAY_ENTRIES-1:0] replay_valid_b0;`

`logic [REPLAY_ENTRIES-1:0] replay_valid_b1;`

Keep the indexed use sites semantically identical: bank-hit compares still
read `replay_valid_b?[replay_lookup_idx]`, fill-bank match checks still read
`replay_valid_b?[replay_fill_idx]`, and live fills still set only the
selected bank's valid bit at `replay_fill_idx`. Reset should clear each
valid vector with a single assignment. Do not pack or otherwise alter the
tag arrays, instruction arrays, MRU array, fill-bank choice, or replay
candidate state in this hypothesis.

This must be bit-equivalent to the current replay predictor. Both replay
banks still store full pc[31:9] tags and instruction words. Both banks are
still compared on every lookahead lookup. Live imem data still has priority
over replay data. The full `replay_cand_pc_q == pc` guard still protects
replay use. The same-entry fill bypass, per-index MRU replacement, static
backward-branch prediction, aligned direct-JAL prediction, JALR EX
resolution, redirect priority, `fetch_ready`, PC update behavior, and
outcome-only prediction metadata remain unchanged.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because table contents,
fill timing, lookup timing, replacement policy, and next-PC decisions do
not change. The intended Pareto win is lower LUT4 and/or higher median Fmax
from reducing reset/index muxing around the replay valid metadata, while
preserving the already-met area target, top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports,
channel-0-only retirement, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder
legality, memory traps, pending-store behavior, divider protocol, and
CoreMark marker behavior.

changes.description:
Change only the replay predictor valid-bit storage shape. Replace the
unpacked `replay_valid_b0` and `replay_valid_b1` arrays with packed
vectors indexed by replay entry. Reset the vectors to zero with vector
assignments, set the selected valid bit on live fills exactly as today,
and keep all existing fill-match, lookup-hit, fill-bypass, MRU,
tag-payload, instruction-payload, candidate-PC guard, live-imem
priority, static branch/direct-JAL prediction, JALR handling, redirect
priority, PC update, and `fetch_ready` behavior unchanged.

Update only IF/ID replay predictor comments to document that replay bank
valid bits are packed vectors, while tag and instruction payload arrays
remain resetless behind those valid bits and MRU replacement remains
separate. Do not change bundle fields, control encodings, decoder
illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the
NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
## Vectorized Replay Valid Banks

### What changed

- Updated `rtl/if_stage.sv` so `replay_valid_b0` and `replay_valid_b1` are packed vectors:
  - `logic [REPLAY_ENTRIES-1:0] replay_valid_b0;`
  - `logic [REPLAY_ENTRIES-1:0] replay_valid_b1;`
- Reset now clears each valid bank with a single vector assignment.
- Existing indexed reads for fill-bank match checks and lookahead hit checks are unchanged.
- Existing live-fill writes still set only the selected bank bit at `replay_fill_idx`.
- Tag arrays, instruction arrays, MRU array, fill-bank choice, fill bypass, replay candidate state, prediction policy, redirect priority, PC update, and `fetch_ready` behavior were left unchanged.
- Updated the IF/ID replay predictor comment in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to document packed valid vectors and resetless tag/instruction payload arrays.

### Deviations

- None. The implementation follows the proposed narrow storage-shape cleanup.

### Concerns

- No RTL concerns identified. This is intended to be bit-equivalent because only the storage shape and reset assignment of the valid metadata changed.

### Local verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: environment-blocked before any checks ran. The script tried to remove staged files under `formal/riscv-formal/cores/auto-arch-researcher`, but `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to `/Users/bonetto/bonetto/auto-arch-tournament/formal/riscv-formal`, outside the writable sandbox roots, so every cleanup `rm` failed with `Operation not permitted`. No formal counterexample was produced.

title: Drop Dead SYSTEM Immediate Decode
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The micro_opt slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto free
win: preserve CoreMark cycles while shaving a small amount of decode logic
or improving median Fmax.

Recent micro_opt experiments show that broad source-shape rewrites in IF,
EX, MEM, the register file, and the ALU are placement-sensitive. The safest
remaining target is a purely local decode simplification whose output is
already architecturally dead. `imm_gen.sv` currently treats SYSTEM
instructions as I-type immediate producers, but the core's decoder accepts
only EBREAK as a legal SYSTEM instruction, and EBREAK does not consume an
immediate, write a register, access memory, branch, or jump. Illegal SYSTEM
encodings also trap without consuming the immediate rail.

Removing SYSTEM from the immediate-generator I-type arm should delete a
redundant opcode match from the ID immediate cone without changing any
pipeline state, control bit, top-level IO, or RVFI-visible behavior.
hypothesis:
In `rtl/imm_gen.sv`, remove opcode `7'b1110011` from the I-type immediate
case. The I-type immediate arm should continue to cover LOAD, OP-IMM, and
JALR, because those instructions actually consume the sign-extended
immediate in EX or MEM. SYSTEM should fall through to the existing default
`imm = 32'b0`.

This is behaviorally safe under the project ISA contract. The only legal
SYSTEM instruction is EBREAK, and EBREAK uses no immediate-derived datapath:
it has no register write, no memory access, no branch/jump redirect, and
retires with `pc_next = pc + 4` through the existing non-control-flow path.
ECALL, CSR, MRET, and all other SYSTEM encodings remain illegal by decoder
default and likewise do not consume the immediate before trapping. RVFI
trap reporting continues to come from decoder legality, not from `imm`.

Do not change `decoder.sv`, the EBREAK-only SYSTEM policy, FENCE behavior,
branch/JAL/JALR prediction or repair, load-use interlocks, forwarding,
pending-store behavior, divider protocol, memory traps, writeback timing,
top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0 retirement ordering, channel-1
tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, or CoreMark timing markers.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because the instruction
stream, stalls, memory behavior, and retirement behavior do not change. The
intended Pareto win is a small LUT4 reduction and/or median Fmax improvement
from eliminating dead SYSTEM immediate decode logic while preserving the
already-met LUT target and every CLAUDE.md invariant.

changes.description:
Remove `7'b1110011` from the I-type immediate case so SYSTEM
instructions use the existing default zero immediate. Preserve the
LOAD, OP-IMM, and JALR I-type immediate behavior, S/B/U/J immediate
generation, module ports, default zero behavior, decoder legality
discipline, EBREAK semantics, and all downstream pipeline behavior.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes Made

- Updated `rtl/imm_gen.sv` so the I-type immediate arm covers only LOAD
  (`7'b0000011`), OP-IMM (`7'b0010011`), and JALR (`7'b1100111`).
- Removed SYSTEM (`7'b1110011`) from that arm. SYSTEM instructions now use
  the existing `default` immediate value of `32'b0`.

## Deviations

- No RTL deviations from the hypothesis.
- The repository-local `bash formal/run_all.sh` invocation could not stage
  files because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared tree outside
  this worktree, and the sandbox denied deleting the stale staged `.sv` files
  there before any proof checks ran.
- To complete the same formal self-check locally, I copied the symlink target
  into `/tmp` as a real writable directory, copied the current `rtl/*.sv`,
  `formal/wrapper.sv`, and `formal/checks.cfg`, and ran the same
  `run_all.sh` flow from that temporary project with only the temporary
  script's `RISCV_FORMAL` path/core name adjusted to avoid the protected
  shared staging directory.

## Concerns

- No behavioral concerns. EBREAK and illegal SYSTEM encodings do not consume
  the immediate rail under the existing decoder/control policy.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` in the worktree: blocked before checks by the
  shared `formal/riscv-formal` symlink staging permissions.
- Equivalent temporary formal self-check: pass, `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Bundle-Stable EX/MEM RD Derivation
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The structural slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto
free win: preserve CoreMark cycles while shaving a little routing, FFs,
or LUT4 without changing packed bundle widths, memory ordering, or the
RVFI contract.

The most successful structural payload cleanup so far was deriving source
register addresses from the carried instruction word instead of transporting
duplicate address rails. The destination register has the same recoverable
shape, but broader attempts that changed every stage's destination payload
were placement-sensitive. The narrower remaining opportunity is the EX/MEM
destination rail only.

`ex_mem_t.rd` is used only as the immediately older producer destination
for EX/MEM forwarding and as the value copied into MEM/WB. The same five
bits are already present in `ex_mem_t.instr[11:7]`, which is carried for
RVFI instruction reporting and trap context. Keeping a separate live EX/MEM
`rd` rail duplicates payload and routing across one stage boundary for no
architectural information gain.
hypothesis:
Keep the `ex_mem_t.rd` field and all packed bundle widths unchanged, but
make the EX/MEM copy a bundle-stability placeholder. In `rtl/ex_stage.sv`,
continue to use the ID/EX destination payload exactly as today where needed
before the EX/MEM boundary, but latch `reg_q.rd <= 5'b0` on every EX/MEM
update, including the normal execute path and the divide-complete path.

Derive all consumers of the EX/MEM destination from the carried instruction
word. In `rtl/core.sv`, feed the forwarding unit's EX/MEM destination input
from `ex_mem_w.instr[11:7]` instead of `ex_mem_w.rd`. In `rtl/mem_stage.sv`,
latch the MEM/WB destination from `in.instr[11:7]` instead of `in.rd`. The
MEM/WB `rd` rail remains semantically live after MEM, so the WB stage, RVFI
destination reporting, and MEM/WB forwarding keep their current shape.

This leaves ID/EX `rd` intact for the load-use interlock and leaves MEM/WB
`rd` intact for final writeback and held MEM/WB forwarding. It changes only
the duplicate EX/MEM transport rail. Forwarding correctness is preserved
because EX/MEM forwarding is still gated by `ex_mem_w.ctrl.reg_write`, so
branch, store, fence, EBREAK, illegal, and invalid-bubble instruction bits
cannot create a false producer. Trapped JAL/JALR instructions already clear
`reg_write` before EX/MEM, so their raw `instr[11:7]` field remains ignored.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because no PC decision,
operand value, branch/JAL/JALR repair, load-use timing, forwarding priority,
divider protocol, pending-store behavior, dmem handshake, writeback timing,
or retirement rule changes. The intended Pareto win is a small LUT4/FF or
median-Fmax improvement from pruning the duplicated EX/MEM destination rail
while preserving the stable bundle topology, top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2
ports, channel-0-only retirement, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`,
decoder illegal-default discipline, memory traps, and CoreMark timing
markers.

changes.description:
Keep the `ex_mem_t.rd` field in the packed bundle, but stop latching a
live destination into it. Drive `reg_q.rd` to `5'b0` in the normal
execute path and in the divide-complete path. Preserve ID/EX `rd`,
branch and jump target arithmetic, misaligned-target trap gating,
divider launch/completion, operand forwarding, EX/MEM valid and stall
behavior, JAL/JALR link-data routing, store-data behavior, and all
RVFI-visible semantics for valid instructions.

Derive the MEM/WB destination from the EX/MEM instruction word by
assigning `reg_q.rd <= in.instr[11:7]` on normal MEM/WB updates instead
of copying `in.rd`. Keep the MEM/WB `rd` field semantically live after
MEM. Preserve load extraction, byte/halfword masks, misalignment traps,
dmem request gating, store-slot enqueue and wait behavior, MEM/WB hold
semantics, RVFI memory payload fields, module ports, and all externally
visible behavior.

Feed the forwarding unit's EX/MEM producer destination from
`ex_mem_w.instr[11:7]` instead of `ex_mem_w.rd`. Leave the ID/EX
load-use destination, MEM/WB forwarding destination, WB write address,
RVFI destination reporting, IF replay wiring, dmem arbitration, RVFI
channel layout, channel-0 order counter, channel-1 tie-off, and the
`core` module IO unchanged.

Update comments only to document that the EX/MEM `rd` field is retained
for bundle-shape stability while EX/MEM forwarding and the MEM/WB
destination are derived from the carried instruction word. Do not
change bundle field names or widths, ALU encodings, decoder illegal
defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the NRET=2
contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changes

- Made the EX/MEM `rd` field a bundle-stability placeholder by driving it to `5'b0` in `rtl/ex_stage.sv` on both the normal execute path and the divide-complete path.
- Changed EX/MEM forwarding in `rtl/core.sv` to derive the producer destination from `ex_mem_w.instr[11:7]` instead of `ex_mem_w.rd`.
- Changed the MEM/WB destination latch in `rtl/mem_stage.sv` to derive `rd` from `in.instr[11:7]` instead of copying `in.rd`.
- Updated the `ex_mem_t` comment in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to document that `rd` is retained only for packed-bundle shape stability while consumers derive the destination from the carried instruction.

## Deviations

- No RTL deviations from the hypothesis. ID/EX `rd` remains live for load-use detection, and MEM/WB `rd` remains live for writeback, RVFI destination reporting, and MEM/WB forwarding.
- The first direct `bash formal/run_all.sh` attempt in the worktree was blocked before any proof ran because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a directory outside the writable sandbox, and the script could not remove staged `.sv` files there. I reran the same script from a `/tmp` copy with a local writable copy of `riscv-formal`; the RTL sources and formal scripts were otherwise unchanged.

## Concerns

- No functional concerns identified. EX/MEM forwarding is still gated by `ex_mem_w.ctrl.reg_write`, and MEM/WB derives the same architectural destination bits from the preserved instruction word.

## Local Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: pass in the `/tmp` writable copy, `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: One-Entry Non-Speculative Return Predictor
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The predictor slot therefore needs a strict Pareto free win:
improve CoreMark throughput without giving back the tight LUT result or
weakening the RVFI contract.

The experiment log makes the predictor boundary clear. The two-bank
instruction replay predictor and the static backward-branch/direct-JAL
policy should stay intact; replay-table reshaping, BHTs, return-stack
target metadata, direct mapping, tag compression, and replacement-policy
changes have generally spent more Fmax or area than they recovered. The
remaining useful predictor gap is JALR returns: direct JAL calls are
predicted in IF, but canonical returns still wait until EX and redirect
there.

A prior four-entry return-address stack reduced CoreMark cycles, but it
carried a predicted target through IF/ID and ID/EX and added enough stack
state and repair logic to lose Fmax and exceed the LUT target. This
hypothesis keeps the useful return-prediction idea but removes the costly
part: use a single non-speculative return target entry and repair in EX by
comparing against the still-stable current return predictor top, not by
carrying a 32-bit predicted-target pipeline payload.
hypothesis:
Add a one-entry non-speculative return predictor for canonical RISC-V
returns. IF should keep the existing static predictor for legal backward
branches and aligned direct JALs, with the same shared static target adder
and the same two-bank replay table. In addition, when the fetched
instruction is a canonical return (`JALR`, `funct3 == 0`, `rd == x0`,
`rs1 == x1` or `x5`, immediate zero) and the one-entry return predictor is
valid, IF predicts the next PC from that stored return address.

Keep updates non-speculative. Do not push or pop in IF. EX should report
resolved, valid, non-trapping call and return events back to IF on internal
wires. Push `pc + 4` when a resolved direct JAL writes x1 or x5. Pop when
a resolved canonical return reaches EX and is not trapping. Because the
push happens after the call reaches EX, very short leaf functions may still
miss, but ordinary returns in CoreMark can be predicted without speculative
rollback machinery.

Do not add a predicted-target field to IF/ID or ID/EX. Instead, expose the
current one-entry return target and valid bit from IF to EX as internal
wires. For a predicted JALR return, EX compares the resolved JALR target
against that current stored target. A predicted return redirects from EX
only when the resolved target differs or the predictor entry is no longer
valid. An unpredicted JALR keeps the existing unconditional EX redirect.
Direct JAL behavior remains unchanged, and conditional branches keep the
existing outcome-only repair.

Misaligned JALR targets remain traps. If a JALR was not predicted, the
existing trap path already leaves the PC sequential. If a canonical return
was predicted from the return predictor but the resolved JALR target is
misaligned, EX must repair the speculative fetch stream back to `pc + 4`
while still marking the instruction as trapping, suppressing the link
write, and reporting `pc + 4` as RVFI `pc_wdata`, exactly as the current
misaligned-control-flow trap semantics require.

This is a single-entry return predictor, not a BHT, BTB, RAS stack, target
cache, or replay-table rewrite. It must not change replay-table geometry,
live-imem priority, fill-bypass behavior, MRU replacement, static
backward-branch prediction, direct-JAL prediction, load-use interlocks,
forwarding priority, pending-store behavior, memory traps, divider
protocol, writeback timing, top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0
retirement ordering, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder
illegal defaults, or CoreMark timing markers.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should improve by removing EX-redirect bubbles
on repeated canonical returns. LUT4 should stay below the current target
because the predictor is only one 32-bit target register, one valid bit,
a canonical-return predecode, and small EX update/repair wiring; it avoids
the 32-bit predicted-target pipeline payload and multi-entry stack that
made the earlier return predictor too expensive.

changes.description:
Add a one-entry non-speculative return predictor. Decode canonical
return instructions in IF and, when the entry is valid, select the
stored return target as the predicted next PC and mark the IF/ID
payload `predicted_taken`. Add internal EX update inputs for direct-JAL
call pushes and canonical-return pops. Expose the current predictor
valid bit and target to EX for repair comparison. Preserve the existing
static branch/direct-JAL predictor, shared `predict_target`, replay
table, live-imem priority, same-entry fill bypass, MRU replacement,
candidate-PC guard, redirect priority, PC update semantics, and
`fetch_ready`.

Add internal return-predictor update outputs: push `pc + 4` for valid,
non-trapping direct JAL calls to x1 or x5, and pop for valid,
non-trapping canonical JALR returns. Gate updates with `!stall` so a
held EX instruction cannot update the predictor repeatedly during a
MEM wait. Modify JALR repair so predicted canonical returns redirect
only when the resolved target differs from the current IF predictor
target, or when a predicted misaligned JALR trap must repair the PC
back to `pc + 4`. Preserve branch repair, direct-JAL behavior,
misaligned-target trap gating, divider launch/completion, operand
forwarding, EX/MEM valid/stall behavior, JAL/JALR link-data routing,
and all RVFI-visible `pc_next` semantics.

Wire the IF-stage return predictor target/valid outputs into EX and
wire the EX-stage push/pop update signals back into IF, without
changing the top-level `core` IO. Preserve IF replay wiring, hazard
and forwarding connections, dmem arbitration, RVFI channel layout,
channel-0 order counter, channel-1 tie-off, and all existing
retirement behavior.

Update comments only, if useful, to document that `predicted_taken`
may also mean a canonical JALR return was predicted by the one-entry
non-speculative return predictor. Do not add predicted-target payload
fields, and do not change bundle field names or widths, control
encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO
assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
## What changed

- Added a one-entry non-speculative return predictor in `rtl/if_stage.sv`.
  - Direct JAL/backward-branch static prediction and the two-bank replay table are otherwise unchanged.
  - IF now predecodes canonical returns (`jalr x0, 0(x1)` / `jalr x0, 0(x5)`) and predicts them from the stored return target when the entry is valid.
  - The return predictor is updated only from EX-provided push/pop wires: push `pc + 4` for resolved direct JAL calls to x1/x5, pop for resolved canonical returns.
  - Return prediction is suppressed in the same cycle as a predictor push or pop so IF cannot use a stale entry while EX is changing the non-speculative top.

- Updated `rtl/ex_stage.sv`.
  - Added direct-JAL call and canonical-return detection.
  - Added non-repeating return predictor push/pop outputs gated by `!stall`, `valid`, and non-trapping control flow.
  - Changed JALR redirect repair so predicted canonical returns redirect only when the resolved target differs from the current IF predictor target or the current entry is invalid.
  - Predicted misaligned canonical returns redirect back to `pc + 4` while preserving the existing trap semantics and RVFI `pc_wdata = pc + 4`.

- Updated `rtl/core.sv` to wire IF predictor valid/target into EX and EX push/pop updates back into IF.

- Updated comments in `rtl/core_pkg.sv` to document that `predicted_taken` can also represent a canonical return prediction. No pipeline payload fields were added.

## Deviations

- Added same-cycle update suppression in IF. The first formal run exposed a `pc_fwd_ch0` counterexample where IF predicted a return with the old one-entry target in the same cycle EX pushed a newer direct-call return address. Because EX later compared against the newer current top, it skipped the required repair. Suppressing prediction during push/pop keeps the predictor non-speculative without carrying a 32-bit predicted target and matches the hypothesis note that very short leaf functions may still miss.

## Concerns

- Very short leaf calls can still miss the return prediction when the return is fetched in the same cycle as the call push, by design.
- Nested calls are only tracked at one level; an inner return pop clears the entry, so an outer return may miss.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh`: attempted, but this sandbox cannot write through `formal/riscv-formal`, which is a symlink to `/Users/bonetto/bonetto/auto-arch-tournament/formal/riscv-formal`; the script failed while removing staged `.sv` files with `Operation not permitted`.
- Equivalent temp-staged formal run in `/tmp` using the same generated checks and channel-1 `PREUNSAT` tally rule: pass, `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Bundle-Stable Load-Only WB Read Data
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. This micro_opt slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto
free win: preserve CoreMark cycles while trimming a small amount of
final-stage routing or improving median Fmax without changing packed
bundle widths.

The recent successful optimization pattern is bundle-stable
constantization: keep the pipeline field layout intact, but stop driving
live values onto rails that are semantically dead for a class of
instructions. The MEM/WB `read_data` rail has that shape. It is selected
by WB only when `ctrl.mem_to_reg` is true, so for ALU ops, jumps, stores,
branches, fences, EBREAK, illegal instructions, and trapping memory ops,
the value latched into `mem_wb_t.read_data` is architecturally ignored.

A broader final-result projection already tried to repurpose MEM/WB result
rails and remove the WB mux; that changed too much placement shape. This
hypothesis is narrower: keep `alu_result`, `read_data`, `ctrl.mem_to_reg`,
`wb_stage`, and every packed field exactly in place, but make the existing
`read_data` payload load-only.
hypothesis:
In `rtl/mem_stage.sv`, keep load extraction, memory masks, dmem request
generation, store-slot behavior, and WB selection unchanged, but qualify
only the MEM/WB `read_data` payload at the register boundary.

Add a local predicate such as:

`load_data_live = mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_read;`

and latch:

`reg_q.read_data <= load_data_live ? load_data : 32'b0;`

on normal MEM/WB updates. Leave `reg_q.alu_result` unchanged, and leave
`wb_stage.sv` unchanged: `w_data` should still be selected by the existing
`in.ctrl.mem_to_reg ? in.read_data : in.alu_result` mux. Legal aligned
loads still write and forward the exact same sign/zero-extended load
result. Non-load instructions and trapping memory operations now carry a
deterministic zero on the dead load-result rail.

Preserve the existing `hold_wb` behavior exactly. During a MEM wait, clear
only `valid` and retain the previous MEM/WB payload, including `read_data`,
so a completed load can still be forwarded through the existing MEM/WB path
to a held dependent instruction. The new zeroing applies only on normal
MEM/WB updates, not during hold cycles.

This is intentionally narrower than a final writeback-result projection.
It does not remove or repurpose fields, does not alter the WB mux, and
does not change RVFI memory payloads. It only removes unnecessary
load-data fanout into a bundle-stable rail when the corresponding control
bit already guarantees the rail will not be consumed.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because no pipeline
state schedule, PC decision, memory-ordering rule, dmem handshake,
forwarding priority, writeback timing, or retirement rule changes. The
intended Pareto win is a small LUT4 reduction and/or higher median Fmax
from constantizing a dead MEM/WB load-result payload on non-load cycles
while preserving top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0 retirement
ordering, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder legality,
memory traps, pending-store behavior, divider protocol, and CoreMark
timing markers.

changes.description:
Add a local load-result-live predicate derived from the existing
legal aligned load condition, for example
`mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_read`. Use it only when updating the
MEM/WB `read_data` field: latch the existing `load_data` for legal
aligned loads and latch `32'b0` otherwise. Preserve load extraction,
byte/halfword masks, misalignment traps, dmem request gating,
pending-store enqueue and wait behavior, MEM/WB hold semantics,
`alu_result`, RVFI memory payload fields, module ports, and all
externally visible behavior.

Update comments only, if useful, to document that
`mem_wb_t.read_data` is retained as the bundle-stable load-result
rail and is meaningful only when `ctrl.mem_to_reg` selects it in WB.
Do not change bundle field names or widths, ALU encodings, decoder
illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO assumptions, or the
NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What Changed

- In `rtl/mem_stage.sv`, added `load_data_live = mem_access && in.ctrl.mem_read`.
- On normal MEM/WB updates, `reg_q.read_data` now latches `load_data` only when `load_data_live` is true; otherwise it latches `32'b0`.
- Preserved the existing `hold_wb` behavior: hold cycles still clear only `valid` and retain the previous MEM/WB payload, including `read_data`.
- Left load extraction, byte/halfword masks, dmem request generation, store-slot enqueue behavior, `alu_result`, WB mux selection, and RVFI memory payload fields unchanged.
- In `rtl/core_pkg.sv`, updated only the `mem_wb_t.read_data` field comment to document it as a load-result rail meaningful when `mem_to_reg` selects it.

## Deviations

- No RTL functional deviations from the hypothesis.
- The direct worktree formal run could not stage files because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared tree outside the writable sandbox; `rm -f` in `formal/run_all.sh` failed with `Operation not permitted` before any proof checks ran.
- To keep the formal self-check meaningful without modifying the protected shared formal tree, I copied `rtl/` and a dereferenced `formal/` tree into `/tmp/hyp-20260429-076-r33s0-formal.MhpSPr` and ran the same `bash formal/run_all.sh` there.

## Concerns

- No known implementation concerns. Non-load and trapping memory instructions now carry zero on the architecturally dead MEM/WB `read_data` rail, while legal loads and held-load forwarding retain the previous behavior.

## Local Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` in the worktree: blocked by sandbox write denial in the shared `formal/riscv-formal` symlink before checks ran.
- `bash formal/run_all.sh` from the writable `/tmp` shadow copy: pass, `Formal: 105 passed, 0 failed`.
- Formal fix attempts used: 0, because no RTL/formal counterexample occurred.

title: Reuse ALU Add For JALR Target
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The structural slot should therefore look for a strict Pareto
free win: preserve CoreMark cycles while reducing duplicated datapath
logic or improving median Fmax without changing packed bundle widths,
memory ordering, or the RVFI contract.

Many recent structural payload and predictor changes were placement
sensitive. The best remaining structural angle is therefore a very local
datapath sharing change whose correctness follows directly from the
existing decode. JALR already enters EX with `alu_src = 1`, `alu_op =
ALU_ADD`, `is_auipc = 0`, and the forwarded rs1 value. That means the
ordinary ALU result for a JALR is already the architectural `rs1 + imm`
sum used to form the JALR target before bit 0 is cleared.

`rtl/ex_stage.sv` still computes a separate `jalr_sum = rs1 + in.imm`
adder in parallel with the ALU. This duplicates a 32-bit add path and
drives the EX redirect/misalignment cone even though the sum is already
available from the ALU for exactly the JALR instruction class. Reusing
the existing ALU add result for JALR target formation removes that
duplicate target adder without changing the pipeline schedule, branch
predictor, store buffer, divider, or retirement boundary.
hypothesis:
In `rtl/ex_stage.sv`, remove the separate JALR target sum adder and derive
the JALR target from the already-computed ALU ADD result. For JALR only,
replace:

`jalr_sum = rs1 + in.imm;`

with use of the combinational `alu_result` value:

`jump_target = in.ctrl.is_jalr ? {alu_result[31:1], 1'b0} : (in.pc + in.imm);`

This is bit-equivalent for valid JALR instructions because the decoder
only marks JALR legal when funct3 is zero, sets `alu_src`, leaves
`is_auipc` clear, and keeps the ALU operation as ADD. The existing EX
operand forwarding muxes therefore feed the same rs1 value into the ALU
that the dedicated `jalr_sum` adder receives today, and the immediate is
the same ID/EX `imm` value.

Keep all non-JALR control-flow target generation unchanged. Conditional
branches and direct JAL still use the existing PC-relative target path,
and JALR still clears bit 0 before redirect or misalignment handling.
JAL/JALR register writeback must continue to use `pc + 4` through the
existing `reg_q.alu_result = in.ctrl.is_jump ? pc+4 : alu_result` path;
the ALU result is reused only as the raw JALR target sum before the
EX/MEM register assignment.

Misaligned target trap behavior remains identical. A JALR whose ALU sum
has bit 1 set still marks the instruction as trapping, suppresses the
redirect, clears the link write, and reports `pc + 4` as RVFI `pc_wdata`.
Direct JAL and branch misalignment checks remain based on their existing
resolved targets.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should remain identical because no PC policy,
prediction policy, load-use interlock, forwarding priority, memory
protocol, divider protocol, writeback timing, or retirement rule changes.
The intended Pareto win is a small LUT4 reduction and/or higher median
Fmax from deleting the duplicate JALR `rs1 + imm` adder while preserving
the stable bundle topology, top-level IO, RVFI NRET=2 ports, channel-0
retirement ordering, channel-1 tie-off, strict `rvfi_order`, decoder
illegal-default discipline, memory traps, and CoreMark timing markers.

changes.description:
Reuse the existing combinational ALU ADD result as the raw JALR target
sum. Remove the dedicated `jalr_sum = rs1 + in.imm` adder and form the
JALR `jump_target` from `{alu_result[31:1], 1'b0}`. Preserve the
PC-relative branch and direct-JAL target path, branch repair,
direct-JAL repair, JALR bit-0 clearing, misaligned-target trap gating,
JAL/JALR `pc + 4` link-data routing, divider launch/completion,
operand forwarding, EX/MEM valid and stall behavior, store-data
behavior, and all RVFI-visible semantics.

Update comments only if useful to document that JALR target formation
reuses the EX ALU ADD result because the decoder already routes JALR
as `rs1 + imm`. Do not change bundle field names or widths, ALU
encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO
assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## Changed

- Updated `rtl/ex_stage.sv` so JALR target formation reuses the existing combinational ALU result.
- Removed the dedicated `jalr_sum = rs1 + in.imm` adder.
- Kept direct JAL and branch target generation PC-relative.
- Kept JAL/JALR register writeback on the existing `pc + 4` path through `reg_q.alu_result`.
- Kept JALR bit-0 clearing and misaligned target trap gating unchanged, now using `{alu_result[31:1], 1'b0}` as the raw JALR target.

## Deviations

- No functional deviations from the hypothesis.
- Did not change `rtl/core_pkg.sv`; the local EX-stage comment is sufficient to document why ALU result reuse is valid.

## Concerns

- No RTL concerns found. This relies on the existing decoder contract that legal JALR enters EX as `ALU_ADD` with `alu_src = 1` and `is_auipc = 0`.

## Verification

- `verilator --lint-only -Wall -Wno-MULTITOP -sv rtl/*.sv`: pass.
- Direct `bash formal/run_all.sh`: blocked by sandbox permissions while removing staged files under `formal/riscv-formal/cores/auto-arch-researcher`; no formal check ran in that invocation.
- Equivalent local formal run from a writable `/tmp` copy of `formal/riscv-formal`, with this worktree's `rtl/` linked in: pass, `105 passed, 0 failed`.

title: Forward-BNE Static Direction Bias
motivation:
The current selected RTL already meets both phase-2 targets: 540.19
CoreMark iter/s versus the 400 target, and 3401 LUT4 versus the 3500
target. The predictor slot therefore needs a strict Pareto free win:
improve CoreMark throughput without disturbing the replay frontend or
giving back the tight LUT result.

The experiment log makes the useful predictor boundary clear. The
two-bank instruction replay predictor and aligned direct-JAL prediction
should stay intact; replay-table reshaping, BHTs, return prediction,
target metadata, direct mapping, tag compression, and replacement-policy
changes generally spent more Fmax or area than they recovered. The only
predictor experiment that showed a clear cycle-count signal was the
funct3-aware static branch bias: it reduced CoreMark cycles, but the
broader BEQ/BNE/compare-branch rewrite hurt placement enough to lose
fitness.

This hypothesis keeps that useful direction signal but narrows it to the
likely high-value case: forward BNE branches. Pure BTFNT predicts all
forward branches not taken, while compiled C often uses forward BNE as a
skip-over or continue-fast-path branch that is taken more often than not.
A BNE-only override avoids changing backward BEQ loop-exit behavior and
avoids adding any table, tag, target storage, update path, or rollback
state.
hypothesis:
Change only the IF-stage static conditional-branch direction rule. Keep
the existing branch opcode check, branch funct3 legality guard, shared
`predict_target` adder, target-alignment check, direct-JAL prediction,
JALR-unpredicted behavior, replay table, live-imem priority, fill bypass,
MRU replacement, candidate-PC guard, redirect priority, `fetch_ready`, and
PC update semantics unchanged.

For legal conditional branches, keep the current backward-taken /
forward-not-taken sign-bit policy except for BNE. A BNE should predict
taken regardless of offset direction; all other legal branch funct3 values
continue to use the existing immediate sign bit. The branch direction can
be expressed as:

`branch_static_taken = fetch_instr[31] || (fetch_instr[14:12] == 3'b001);`

With the existing `branch_funct_legal` guard, reserved funct3 values 010
and 011 remain unpredicted and still trap through the normal decoder/RVFI
path. BEQ keeps the current sign-bit policy, so this is narrower than the
previous full equality-branch polarity experiment. BLT, BGE, BLTU, and
BGEU also keep the current BTFNT behavior.

EX repair remains the existing outcome-only comparison between the resolved
branch outcome and `in.predicted_taken`, so any static-policy mistake is an
ordinary misprediction and cannot affect architectural correctness. Direct
JAL behavior, JALR behavior, replay behavior, memory ordering, divider
behavior, RVFI ordering, and top-level IO are unchanged.

CoreMark iterations/cycle should improve if the cycle gain from the earlier
funct3-aware experiment came from hot forward BNE branches. LUT4 and Fmax
should be much less exposed than the broader rule because this adds only a
small BNE override to the existing sign-bit predicate and no predictor
state. The intended phase-2 result is higher CoreMark iter/s with LUT4 no
worse than the current 3401 result.

changes.description:
Add a local `branch_static_taken` signal for the static conditional
branch predictor. Compute it as the existing sign-bit rule OR a BNE
override, for example `fetch_instr[31] || (fetch_instr[14:12] == 3'b001)`.
Replace the current `fetch_instr[31]` direction term in
`branch_predict_taken` with this signal. Preserve the branch opcode
gate, branch funct3 legality check, target-alignment guard, shared
`predict_target` adder, direct-JAL prediction, JALR-unpredicted
behavior, `fetch_kill` gating, redirect priority, replay lookup PC
selection, live-imem priority, replay candidate guard, fill bypass,
MRU replacement, PC update semantics, `fetch_ready`, and IF/ID payload
fields.

Update comments only, if useful, to document that `predicted_taken` for
conditional branches uses the static BTFNT sign-bit rule with a narrow
BNE-taken override, while direct JAL prediction and replay-table
behavior remain unchanged. Do not change bundle fields, control
encodings, decoder illegal defaults, RVFI field widths, top-level IO
assumptions, or the NRET=2 contract.

implementation_notes:
# Implementation Notes

## What changed

- Updated `rtl/if_stage.sv` to add `branch_static_taken`.
- Conditional branch prediction now uses `fetch_instr[31] || (fetch_instr[14:12] == 3'b001)` under the existing branch opcode, legal-funct3, predict-enable, and target-alignment gates.
- Updated `rtl/core_pkg.sv` comments to document that conditional branch `predicted_taken` uses BTFNT with a narrow BNE-taken override.

## Deviations

- No functional deviation from the hypothesis. Replay lookup/fill behavior, JAL prediction, JALR non-prediction, redirect priority, PC update semantics, and IF/ID payload fields were left unchanged.

## Concerns

- No RTL concerns found.
- The direct worktree invocation of `bash formal/run_all.sh` could not stage files in this sandbox because `formal/riscv-formal` is a symlink to a shared repository outside the writable roots. It failed before generating formal checks with `Operation not permitted` while removing stale staged `.sv` files.

## Local verification
